When transitioning from random-negative pretraining to hard-negative focused training, anneal the influence of static negatives or gradually increase K to avoid abrupt shifts in gradient statistics. STAR’s α-blend—mixing a small portion of random/in-batch loss into the static-hard-negative loss—is an example of a practical curriculum that prevents pathological domination by a few difficult queries ([41]).

Some practical numeric heuristics that have worked in the literature: in ADORE-style settings, top-200 hard negatives were effective in TREC-scale experiments; STAR commonly recommends α in [0.05, 0.2] as a default random-mix proportion; local-first disagreement mining can be run daily or per-ingest batch for small stores (tens of thousands of chunks), and modest fine-tuning schedules (e.g., a few epochs with small learning rates) provide outsized returns for small embedders ([41], [36]).

Evaluation caveats matter. Heterogeneous benchmarks and pooling biases (documented in [39]) complicate cross-system claims: retrievers tuned on MS MARCO or with particular chunking/tokenization choices may behave very differently on zero-shot splits. The same is true for late-interaction systems where index size, tokenization, and compression interact tightly with training recipes ([37]). Therefore, when reporting results from ANCE, STAR/ADORE, or disagreement mining, always publish index refresh cadence, K for hard negatives, PQ/compression parameters, chunking and tokenization, and the detailed makeup of your negative stack to enable fair comparisons.

Negative sampling strategies also influence downstream system behaviors. Rerankers and cross-encoders can correct some retrieval errors but add latency and complexity; [36] documents that naive post-hoc re-ranking can sometimes hurt heterogeneous-metric performance and complicate on-device deployments. Late-interaction distillation, as in [37], requires careful negative design because teacher quality and negative choice strongly affect distilled token geometry. Embedding quality and negative-signal availability interact: stronger base models generate fewer disagreement triples, so disagreement mining has diminishing returns as base embedding quality increases; conversely, it is particularly beneficial for small or domain-specialized models.

Safety, auditing and operational robustness must be part of the negative-mining design. Pipelines that rely on asynchronous infrastructure (ANCE-style) or on per-step dynamic retrieval require robust provenance, index-versioning, and audit logs so that retrieval-behavior changes are traceable and reversible. Automated fine-tuning with self-supervised signals (e.g., disagreement triples or experience-based updates) should be instrumented with quarantine and rollback mechanisms to prevent noisy synthetic datasets from corrupting embedding geometry irreversibly ([10], [12], [25]). Canary index refreshes, A/B tests, and human-in-the-loop checks are prudent guardrails before wide index swaps.

In operational terms, the following decision flow is practical:

- At web scale with a substantial compute budget: warm-start with BM25 or teacher-derived static negatives, adopt STAR-style stabilization early, then move to ADORE-style query-dynamic negatives against a fixed precomputed index using LambdaLoss to directly optimize top-K. Reserve full-corpus ANCE-style re-encodes for cases where encoder changes are expected to yield large gains and the re-encode cost can be amortized ([40], [41]).

- If compute is limited but a modest ANN index exists: use STAR’s stabilization recipe (warm-start static negatives and mix a controlled fraction of random/in-batch negatives), and consider ADORE-style per-step retrieval if you can precompute and compress document embeddings ([41]).

- For local-first, privacy-sensitive, or small-model settings: favor disagreement mining as a first-line, low-cost self-supervision source for hard negatives; train with MNRL and in-batch negatives, validate with heterogeneous evaluations like BEIR, and monitor for distributional blind spots such as FiQA-style colloquial queries ([36], [39]).

Near-term research directions that would materially reduce operational cost include more efficient selective re-encoding schemes that capture ANCE benefits with reduced inferencer cost (e.g., cluster-centroid updates or prioritized per-document re-encoding), better quantification and calibration of disagreement-mined triples across diverse user distributions, and standardized reporting of negative-stack details and index-refresh cadences so comparisons include index-maintenance costs rather than only final nDCG numbers. The field would benefit from reproducible baselines that make the operational cost of negative mining explicit.

The conceptual thrust of the literature is consistent: align negative sampling to the ranking metric you care about, stabilize static approximations with random diversity to avoid pathological domination, and obtain dynamic negatives cheaply where possible by fixing the side of the model you can precompute (documents) or by mining inexpensive self-supervised signals (disagreement). Combining these ideas into principled engineering recipes yields robust gains across both large-scale and local-first regimes while keeping operational cost and safety manageable ([40], [41], [36], [37], [39]).

### 6.4. Scaling limits, concurrency and runtime bottlenecks
Scaling ambitious memory- and retrieval-driven agent systems from research prototypes to production deployments exposes practical bottlenecks that are often invisible in small-scale academic evaluations but dominate real-world cost, latency and safety trade-offs. Below I synthesize how three interacting axes—indexing and ingestion scale, concurrent runtime workloads (including multi-agent communication and KV-cache reuse), and constrained-device/on-device trade-offs—create recurrent failure modes and engineering tensions. For each class of bottleneck I summarize evidence from the literature, quantify costs where available, and present mitigation patterns that have proven effective or follow naturally from the surveyed architectures. The aim is to provide principled engineering patterns (time-windowed shards, lazy re-rank cascades, incremental re-encoding, robust checkpointing) that reconcile performance, resource limits and attack-surface concerns in production settings.

Practical bottlenecks: what breaks first and why
- Offline embedding and index construction are expensive and brittle. Dense architectures shift cost from query-time to offline embedding computation and index building. DPR documents this trade-off concretely: computing embeddings for millions of passages required many GPU-hours, and FAISS index builds for large collections are nontrivial, so “rebuilds” become costly whenever encoders change ([75]). Iterative training schemes that require re-encoding (e.g., ANCE-style negative mining) compound this expense by periodically re-encoding the corpus for fresh hard negatives ([40]). In production, background index work can become the dominant recurring expense and a scaling chokepoint when models are updated frequently.

- ANN index rebuilds and refresh cadence collide with availability and latency constraints. Many ANN indexes are fast for queries but costly to rebuild or to update with high fidelity. Naïve full-index re-encodes cause long wall-times and disruptive write windows; DPR and ANCE highlight hours-long build times and the operational trade-offs between freshness and compute ([75], [40]).

- High-dimensional or multi-vector indexes stress GPU memory and I/O. Late-interaction and token-level multi-vector representations improve quality but inflate storage and memory footprints by an order of magnitude compared to single-vector bi-encoders. Compression and denoising reduce this burden, but the fundamental trade-off remains: more representational granularity yields better accuracy at the cost of increased GPU memory and higher-bandwidth indexing/serving tiers ([37]).

- Concurrency and single-writer-store constraints bottleneck ingestion-heavy systems. Local-first single-file stores (e.g., SQLite + FTS5) are attractive for privacy and simple deployment, but single-writer semantics become a scalability bottleneck under concurrent writes or high ingest rates. The local-first literature documents these operational limits and the trade-offs between single-node guarantees and multi-client concurrency ([36]).

- Multi-agent systems introduce runtime bottlenecks: prefill costs, KV-shuffling and offload bandwidth. Reusing KV caches (latent exchanges) can yield dramatic speedups but requires homogeneous models, compatible RoPE/positional encodings, and careful handling of positional offsets. KVCOMM demonstrates large wall-time savings under these assumptions but also reveals fragility when agents are heterogeneous or prefixes shift ([29]).

- Re-rankers and cross-encoders are costly at scale; lazy strategies are essential. Cross-encoder re-rankers can improve quality but impose per-candidate quadratic costs that scale poorly. Systems therefore rely on fast ANN stages and apply heavy re-rankers selectively to a narrow top-K. Naïve always-on cross-encoder reranking is often wasteful and may not produce commensurate end-to-end gains in all datasets ([75], [36]).

Why these bottlenecks matter for agentic systems
Agentic systems compose retrieval with long-horizon planning, tool execution and multi-agent coordination, so bottlenecks interact and amplify. An expensive index rebuild coinciding with a model update can delay safe rollouts; eager cross-encoder re-ranks increase per-step latency and amplify drift if their judgments are noisy; optimistic KV reuse accelerates progress but can introduce silent correctness gaps and security exposures when latent channels lack provenance. Benchmarks that stress trajectory-level interactions and adversarial settings make clear that scaling decisions affect throughput, cost and systemic vulnerability ([13], [21], [35]).

Patterns and mitigations: architectural and operational remedies
Below are practical mitigations mapped to the failure modes above, with pointers to empirical evidence and to the architectures surveyed earlier.

1) Time-windowed shards, single-writer avoidance and write-path scaling
Problem: Single-file stores (e.g., SQLite/FTS5) choke under concurrent writes. Local-first systems are particularly vulnerable ([36]).

Mitigations:
- Time-windowed shards and append-only segments. Partition writes into hourly/daily shards that are append-only; run asynchronous compaction/merge jobs to consolidate shards and update recall indices. This preserves single-file simplicity for reads while distributing writes and deferring heavy merging to off-peak windows.
- Leader-and-replica write lanes. Use a short-lived leader pool to serialize commits while many readers access replicas, minimizing the single-writer window and enabling horizontal read scalability.
- Lightweight write batching and backpressure. Batch small writes into larger transactions and apply backpressure to upstream agents to reduce I/O overhead and allow background indexers to keep up.
- Explicit concurrency budgets and graceful degradation. When ingest exceeds budgets, shed noncritical writes (debug traces, low-utility examples) to preserve core memory integrity. Integrity checking and schema versioning are operational best practices ([36]).

Rationale: Sharding aligns naturally with incremental index maintenance: each shard can be re-encoded independently, enabling partial rebuilds rather than global rebuilds.

2) Incremental re-encoding, dual-index swaps and background rebuilds
Problem: Full-corpus re-encoding for encoder updates or ANCE-style negative mining is prohibitive ([75], [40]).

Mitigations:
- Incremental re-encoding with dual-index swap. Maintain an active index serving queries and a staging index being updated; atomically swap the staging index into production when ready. This reduces downtime and lets queries continue against a stable index.
- Time-windowed incremental encoding. Re-encode only recent or high-change shards; leave older shards unchanged longer when they are unlikely to affect top-K retrieval.
- Staged ANN update strategies. Use an asynchronous inferencer to refresh embeddings on a rolling basis but constrain cadence and scope (e.g., re-encode high-traffic or high-change documents) to bound compute.
- Delta-indexing and residual storage. Store small residuals or projection corrections that approximate a new encoder’s vectors without full re-encodes; use them temporarily until a full re-encode completes.
- Robust checkpointing and resumable builds. Design index pipelines to checkpoint fine-grained progress so builds can resume across failures.

Rationale: DPR’s engineering notes motivate asynchronous and incremental strategies to keep model-update cycles practical while preserving training dynamics.

3) Lazy re-rankers, cascaded scoring and “pay-for-quality” retrieval
Problem: Heavy re-rankers are expensive and can cause latency spikes when applied broadly.

Mitigations:
- Cascaded retrieval: ANN → lightweight re-scorer → heavyweight cross-encoder. Use a cheap ANN to retrieve a broad candidate set, prune with a shallow re-scorer or compact late-interaction model, and apply a heavyweight cross-encoder only to the top candidates or high-value queries.
- Query-adaptive re-ranking budgets. Allocate re-ranker effort based on query uncertainty or predicted value; increase budget for ambiguous or high-stakes queries.
- Lazy background re-ranking (asynchronous). Return fast approximate results to enable forward progress, while asynchronously computing a higher-quality re-ranked list for later critical steps.
- On-device mini-rerankers. Use distilled cross-encoders or compact late-interaction models locally and escalate to server-side re-rankers only when necessary ([36]).

Rationale: Benchmarks and engineering work show late-interaction and cross-encoders give high zero-shot quality but at significant compute cost; treat re-ranking as a conditional resource to be spent selectively ([75], [39], [37]).

4) Index compression, multi-vector trade-offs and GPU memory management
Problem: Multi-vector representations increase storage and GPU memory requirements.

Mitigations:
- Product quantization and centroid+residual compression. Apply PQ and centroid+residual compression to late-interaction token vectors to shrink footprints while preserving most quality ([37]).
- Aggressive residual sparsification and token aggregation. Group token embeddings into centroids and store residuals for fine detail to reduce storage and speed lookups.
- Memory-aware index layouts and sharding. Keep hot shards (recent/high-traffic) in GPU-resident indexes and store cold shards on CPU-backed ANN structures with staged prefetching.
- Hybrid small-model tuning. When device constraints preclude heavy multi-vector models, fine-tune and compress small embedders to achieve acceptable on-device quality ([36]).

Rationale: ColBERTv2 validates that compression and denoising can capture much of late-interaction’s benefit with much smaller footprints, making deployments in constrained GPU fleets feasible.

5) Hybrid lexical + dense fusion and rare-surface-form rescue
Problem: Dense retrievers can miss rare surface-form signals, reducing recall for name- or phrase-sensitive queries.

Mitigations:
- Hybrid fusion with adaptive per-query weighting. Fuse dense and lexical results via Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) with per-query IDF weighting to prioritize lexical signals when surface-form tokens are discriminative.
- Disagreement-driven negative mining. Mine dense/lexical disagreements as cheap hard negatives to train small embedders, avoiding full ANN re-encodes ([36], [40]).
- Query-triggered lexical fallback. For queries with exact-match tokens or high IDF terms, prioritize lexical retrieval or expand re-ranker budgets for lexical candidates.

Rationale: DPR and BEIR analyses emphasize BM25’s complementarity to dense retrievers; hybrid strategies operationalize that complementarity for robustness ([75], [39]).

6) KV-cache reuse, RoPE-aware engineering and fallbacks for heterogeneity
Problem: KV reuse yields large savings only under strong assumptions (homogeneous backbones, consistent RoPE). When assumptions break, reuse can be unsafe ([29], [6]).

Mitigations:
- RoPE-aware anchor pools and de-rotation. Store anchor caches and apply RoPE de-rotation/interpolation to adapt caches to shifted prefixes, as in KVCOMM.
- Controlled adapter layers for heterogeneity. Insert small conversion adapters or per-layer projection matrices to map past_key_values between different model coordinate systems.
- Fallback partial prefill and partial re-encode. When conversion error exceeds thresholds, prefill only top-k recent tokens and let normal decoding handle the remainder.
- Explicit auditing and provenance for latent channels. Complement latent exchanges with short textual summaries or signed provenance tokens so latent channels remain auditable ([12], [6]).

Rationale: KVCOMM shows anchor-driven reuse yields substantial prefill speedups with acceptable accuracy on homogeneous models but documents fragility when prefixes shift; robust mismatch detection and conservative fallbacks are therefore essential.

7) Production index maintenance: robust checkpointing, integrity tests and versioning
Problem: Index builds and re-encodes fail in the wild, and partial or corrupted indexes can silently degrade quality or cause safety incidents.

Mitigations:
- Fine-grained checkpointing and streaming writes. Break builds into many small, atomically commit-able units so interrupted builds can resume quickly.
- Integrity tests and canary queries. Continuously run representative canaries against candidate indices to detect regressions before promotion.
- Versioned indices and metadata manifests. Attach manifests to index artifacts describing encoder checksum, quantization parameters and build hyperparameters to enable reproducible rollbacks.
- Index-level provenance and auditable swap logs. Log provenance tokens linked to index commits, especially when index updates are tied to high-privilege agent actions ([35], [18]).

Rationale: Robust index maintenance reduces MTTR and prevents silent quality regressions; adversarial and long-horizon benchmarks emphasize that auditability and rollback capabilities materially improve operational safety.

8) Cost‑accuracy trade-offs for on‑device deployments: small embedder engineering and adaptive fusion
Problem: On-device memory and compute are tight; heavy multi-vector retrievers and cross-encoders are usually infeasible locally.

Mitigations:
- Train compact embedders with disagreement-mined supervision. Disagreement mining between lightweight dense and lexical retrievals yields hard negatives that enable small embedders to approach larger-model quality ([36]).
- Hybrid local+cloud cascades. Use a local retriever for the common case and fall back to cloud re-rankers or larger late-interaction models for ambiguous or high-value queries.
- Adaptive RRF weighting per query. Use per-query IDF-weighted RRF to balance lexical and dense signals without heavy compute.
- Quantized on-device models and distilled rerankers. Deploy distilled cross-encoders and quantized re-rankers that fit on-device but retain some server-side precision benefits.

Rationale: Benchmarks and experiments show well-tuned small models with thoughtful negative mining and fusion often outperform naive upscaling for on-device scenarios ([76], [36]).

9) Operationalizing safety under scale: staged verification and lazy enforcement
Problem: Scaling defenses (memory admission checks, tool pre-execution guards) across many agents can become a throughput bottleneck.

Mitigations:
- Two-tiered guards: fast conservative checks + deeper async analysis. Deploy a cheap, high-recall filter to block obvious dangers and schedule expensive, higher-fidelity checks asynchronously. This guide-and-correct pattern reduces false positives while avoiding runtime blocking ([10]).
- Consensus and lesson memories for admission. Use ensemble consensus checks or multi-path reasoning before admitting memories; quarantine risky updates as “lessons” and consult them before future admissions ([10], [12]).
- Judge ensembles with stratified human audits. Calibrate diverse LLM judges with periodic human samples and route only low-confidence or high-stakes decisions to human auditors. Trajectory and robustness benchmarks show this reduces attacker success rates while controlling cost ([13], [21]).

Rationale: Layered guard architectures deliver a better utility-safety trade-off than monolithic abort-first defenses; for long-horizon agents, guidance-and-correction reduces benign friction while catching sophisticated attacks in offline verification passes ([10]).

Case studies and cross-cutting examples
- Dense retrieval at scale. DPR quantifies offline vs. online cost: expensive offline embeddings and FAISS builds yield high query throughput and strong end-to-end QA when paired with a reader ([75]). A practical production point is ANN for fast recall, distilled late-interaction for mid-tier re-ranking, and a cross-encoder for critical queries.
- Local-first hybrid retrieval. [36] shows disagreement-driven negatives plus per-query adaptive RRF produce strong BEIR performance while remaining auditable on-device; it also addresses single-writer constraints with schema versioning and integrity checks.
- KV reuse for multi-agent MAS. [29] achieves substantial prefill speedups via RoPE-aware anchors and interpolation but documents failure modes when offsets change or agents are heterogeneous; mismatch detection and conservative fallbacks are therefore required.
- Compression and late interaction. [37] validates denoising and residual compression as a pathway to preserve late-interaction benefits with reduced index footprints.
- Safety-first memory pipelines. [10] and trajectory-focused benchmarks demonstrate that consensus-based validation and staged admission rules vastly reduce memory-poisoning risks while keeping throughput acceptable.

Open engineering research directions
- Incremental ANN algorithms with provable consistency bounds for partial re-encoding to capture ANCE benefits with reduced inferencer cost.
- Lightweight, certified adapters for KV-cache interoperability across heterogeneous backbones (RoPE variants and different layer counts) with calibrated fidelity metrics.
- Techniques for index delta-encoding and residual approximations that cheaply map older embeddings to newer encoder spaces and reduce full re-encode frequency.
- Operational benchmarks that jointly measure throughput, index-rebuild cost and safety impact (combining index cost, QPS, re-ranker latency and adversarial success rates). Existing efforts provide slices, but a unified deployment-level harness would enable apples-to-apples trade-offs.

Conclusion: reconcile efficiency, correctness and auditability
Scaling these systems is not merely a matter of more compute; it requires controlling interacting levers—index freshness, re-ranker budgets, KV-reuse fidelity and write-path topology—that affect safety and auditability. Practical systems therefore combine:
- sharded, time-windowed ingestion and incremental re-encoding to bound rebuild cost;
- cascaded lazy re-ranking and query-adaptive budgets to control per-query latency;
- compression and hybrid fusion to balance memory and accuracy;
- KV reuse with RoPE-aware alignment and conservative heterogeneity fallbacks; and
- robust checkpointing, canary testing and provenance logging to reduce MTTR and enable safe rollbacks.

These operational patterns follow directly from the literature: from DPR’s accounting of index costs and FAISS engineering to the single-file integrity checks in [36], from RoPE-aware anchors in [29] to compression advances in [37]. When combined with safety-first runtime practices (staged guards and consensus lessons from [10] and adversarial suites like [13], [21], [35]), these patterns let practitioners operate high-throughput, reliable agentic systems that are responsive, auditable and resilient under continual model evolution.

Ultimately, the field needs practical, composable engineering toolkits and deployment-level benchmarks that measure the real cost of correctness and safety. Work that targets this gap—disagreement-mined local embedder training ([36]), anchor-based KV reuse ([29]), and compressed late-interaction schemes ([37])—points the way toward balanced production stacks that trade compute dollars for robustness in controlled, auditable ways. Complemented by red-team frameworks ([18], [35]) and execution-grounded feedback loops in domain-specific agents (e.g., [32], [63]), the community is converging on repeatable operational patterns to move retrieval- and memory-driven agents from brittle prototypes to dependable systems.

### 6.5. Evaluation of retrieval improvements and known negative results
The last several years have seen rapid diversification in retrieval strategies for LLM-augmented systems: dense dual-encoders, late‑interaction retrievers, pragmatic local‑first stores, and hybrid lexical/dense fusion. Empirical results are mixed. Some interventions yield large, robust gains in end‑to‑end utility, while other plausible fixes provide only marginal improvements or actively degrade downstream metrics. Below we synthesize the most consequential ablations and negative findings from the recent literature, explain why some approaches work better than others, and give concrete evaluation and reporting recommendations to make retrieval-enabled agents easier to compare and deploy.

1. What actually moves the needle: embeddings and negatives versus post‑hoc tricks
Two consistent findings across retrieval work are (a) the primacy of embedding quality and (b) the outsized effect of well‑chosen negatives during training. Improving the embedding space or the negative sampling regimen typically yields larger and more robust gains than applying heavy post‑hoc re‑ranking or heuristic boosts.

- Embedding quality is the primary lever. Dense dual‑encoders demonstrated early that a relatively small, well‑trained embedding model can dramatically change recall and end‑to‑end QA performance: a learned dual‑encoder substantially outperformed BM25 after modest supervision (thousands of labeled pairs) in retrieved‑reader pipelines ([75]). Large embedding benchmarks confirm this: an embedder’s inductive biases and supervision produce large, sometimes dominant, effects on downstream metrics ([76]). There is no universally dominant embedding; performance varies by task, so investing in task‑appropriate tuning (or broad, strong embeddings) is generally the best first step.

- Curated negatives and dynamic mining outperform naïve re‑rankers. The ANCE paradigm showed that mining realistic hard negatives from a moving ANN index and using them during training reduces train/test mismatch and yields robustness gains ([40]). Subsequent work argues static hard negatives can destabilize learning; hybrid schedules that mix static, random and dynamic negatives or on‑the‑fly schemes tend to align training better with final ranking objectives ([41]). Because these methods change the top‑K candidate pool — the upstream recall substrate available to any downstream reader or re‑ranker — they often produce outsized end‑to‑end effects. In practice, disagreement‑mining between lexical and dense runs can create cheap, high‑quality negatives that substantially improve small embedders ([36]).

2. Negative and null results that should temper intuition
Alongside clear successes, a set of negative findings provides important guardrails. These results explain why popular heuristics or “obvious” pipelines sometimes fail in benchmarks or production.

- Cross‑encoder re‑rankers can degrade end‑to‑end utility. It is common to apply a strong cross‑encoder to a small candidate set returned by a fast ANN. However, careful ablations show this sometimes hurts end‑to‑end metrics or yields negligible utility relative to its cost. For example, after embedding fine‑tuning and adaptive RRF fusion, adding a cross‑encoder did not improve NDCG on the tested BEIR subsets and often only increased latency and cost ([36]). This aligns with BEIR-era observations: re‑rankers and late‑interaction models can be effective zero‑shot but are computationally heavy and susceptible to mismatches arising from upstream chunking and candidate distributions ([39]). If the candidate pool does not include the truly useful passages, a stronger re‑ranker cannot recover them and may overfit to superficial lexical patterns among the candidates.

- Frequency and recency boosts often harm benchmark performance. Preferring frequent or recently seen documents can help personalization and freshness, but in pooled‑relevance evaluations such boosts can bias retrieval toward superficial platform artifacts and away from annotated relevance. The local‑first work reports that simple frequency/recency heuristics failed to improve NDCG on BEIR‑derived suites and in some cases degraded it ([36]). Frequency boosts can be useful product signals, but they must be validated with pooled human relevance checks; otherwise they risk optimizing for internal logs instead of human relevance.

- Static hard negatives and stale index refresh introduce instability. ANCE’s gains require periodic full‑corpus re‑encoding to refresh negatives; static hard negatives derived once can cause overfitting and instability because the model learns idiosyncrasies in the negative pool ([41]). ANCE addresses this with expensive refreshes ([40]), while hybrid methods (disagreement mining, STAR/ADORE‑style schedules) trade off cost and negative representativeness. Negative sampling therefore requires cadence and monitoring; it is not a one‑time engineering sunk cost.

3. Late‑interaction and multi‑vector architectures: quality vs. footprint
Late‑interaction models often yield strong zero‑shot performance, but they impose significant storage and memory costs.

- Late‑interaction excels in quality but costs space. BEIR showed late‑interaction and re‑ranking architectures frequently achieve the best zero‑shot quality across diverse domains while incurring high indexing and serving costs ([39]). Engineering work on compression demonstrates practical mitigations: ColBERTv2 shows that residual compression and denoised supervision can reduce late‑interaction footprints by roughly 6–10× while preserving much of the quality advantage ([37]). When budgets permit, a compressed late‑interaction model is a pragmatic option; when budgets are constrained, invest first in better embeddings and negatives.

- Multi‑vector complexity vs. single‑vector efficiency. Token‑level or multi‑vector representations capture fine‑grained relevance but dramatically inflate index size. For device‑constrained or local‑first systems, a well‑tuned single‑vector embedder with careful fusion (lexical+dense) often delivers a competitive middle ground ([36]). The practical engineering recommendation is to profile: compare a tuned single‑vector baseline with compressed multi‑vector options under real latency and memory constraints, and measure end‑to‑end task utility rather than relying on isolated ranking scores.

4. Hybrid lexical + dense fusion: adaptive per‑query weighting and disagreement signals matter
Hybrid pipelines that combine BM25 and dense retrieval often outperform either alone, but only when fusion is adaptive and per‑query characteristics are respected.

- BM25 remains a strong baseline for rare surface forms. BEIR highlighted that BM25 is robust across many domains (technical, biomedical, legal) where lexical matches matter ([39]). Hybrid systems should allocate more lexical weight when queries contain discriminative surface tokens, for example via per‑query IDF weighting or adaptive RRF.

- Disagreement‑based negatives provide a cheap supervision signal. Mining disagreements between vector and lexical runs near the top‑K produces high‑quality hard negatives without expensive global ANN refreshes. The local‑first pipeline shows that training on disagreement‑mined triples improves NDCG across many BEIR tasks and yields a strong small embedder ([36]). This is a practical pattern when compute budgets preclude full‑corpus negative refresh.

5. Ablations: what was tried and what did not help
Across multiple studies, several interventions consistently failed to help or actively harmed performance; these constitute useful empirical priors.

- Cross‑encoder reranking after careful embedding tuning often yielded no gain. In the local‑first ablations, embedding fine‑tuning plus adaptive RRF fusion removed any benefit from adding a cross‑encoder; the re‑ranker added latency and cost without improving NDCG ([36]). This suggests re‑rankers are not a substitute for better embeddings.

- Frequency/recency heuristics degraded pooled metrics. Simple boosts based on interaction frequency or recency hurt pooled evaluation NDCG in some settings ([36]). These heuristics should be validated separately for product UX versus benchmark performance.

- Heavy‑weight negative sampling destabilized learning when static. Repeatedly reusing identical hard negatives can induce overfitting; mixing in random negatives or adopting dynamic refresh schedules is necessary for stability ([41], [40]).

6. Engineering and operational ablations: index size, refresh cadence, and hidden knobs
Retrieval systems are full‑stack artifacts. Several operational parameters, if unreported, make comparisons misleading.

- Index size and compression choices matter. Papers like ColBERTv2 quantify space savings from centroid+residual compression and demonstrate large storage efficiency gains with limited quality loss ([37]). Local‑first systems must report single‑file footprints, WAL settings, and other filesystem semantics ([36]). Index sizes, PQ settings, and residual budgets directly constrain deployability.

- Negative‑mining and index‑rebuild cadence change training dynamics. ANCE requires periodic re‑encoding; the refresh interval and wall‑clock compute must be reported because stale refreshes alter training hardness ([40]). Disagreement and local mining shift this trade‑off and should publish their sampling budgets.

- Query‑time fusion and per‑query weighting are sensitive knobs. Adaptive RRF with per‑query IDF weighting can substantially improve NDCG; static fusion masks available gains. Report per‑query fusion rules and calibration methods ([36]).

7. Pooling, label sparsity and the danger of under‑pooling
A frequent methodological failure is inadequate pooling. BEIR showed that pools biased toward lexical retrievers miss many dense improvements ([39]).

- Pooling expansions and pooled re‑annotation. Benchmarks should pool candidates from a variety of retrievers (lexical, dense, late‑interaction, re‑rankers) and re‑annotate long‑tail candidates. Without pooled re‑annotation, a model surfacing semantically better but lexically different passages will be penalized. If full re‑annotation is infeasible, adopt stratified human auditing (e.g., audit 5–10% of re‑ranked candidates) and publish the pooled candidate lists as part of reproducible artifacts.

8. Practical ablation patterns and recommendations for reproducible evaluation
To make results comparable and actionable, adopt standardized ablations and reporting.

- Report embedding and training details. Disclose base checkpoints, embedding dimensions, projection heads, finetuning losses (e.g., MultipleNegativesRankingLoss), batch sizes, and exact negative‑sampling strategies (in‑batch, BM25, ANN, disagreement), including refresh cadence and compute budget ([36], [40], [41]).

- Report index footprint, compression and latency under realistic load. Include uncompressed and compressed sizes, PQ/quantization settings, multi‑vector counts, memory footprint, and median/95th‑percentile latency at representative corpus sizes ([37], [36], [29]).

- Describe fusion and re‑rank settings and ablate embedding‑only vs embedding+fusion vs embedding+re‑rank. Provide RRF parameters, candidate set sizes, and the cross‑encoder architecture/cost when used ([36], [37]).

- Disclose pooling procedures and release pooled candidate lists when possible. Follow BEIR’s heterogeneous pooling practice ([39]).

- Report end‑to‑end task metrics, not isolated ranking scores. Evaluate retrieval within the downstream stack (reader+retriever) because improved NDCG does not always translate to higher downstream task success ([75]).

- Publish operational budgets and cadences. State index refresh schedules, negative‑mining cadence, memory admission budgets, compaction/pruning cadences, and human auditing budgets ([40], [36]).

9. Beyond IR metrics: monitoring for misevolution and downstream harm
Retrieval interacts with memory systems, lesson loops, and on‑the‑fly caches. Several works in embodied and memory research highlight risks from unvalidated writes and the need for admission filters and provenance ([26], [25], [4], [5]).

- Enforce admission protocols for new memories. When retrieved content is used to update long‑term memory or lessons, validate candidate memories via sandboxed checks, simulation, consensus validators, or human review before adding them to semantic stores. Self‑evolving memory pipelines must publish these filters; failing to do so is a reproducibility gap ([26], [25], [4]).

- Monitor retrieval drift and provide rollback mechanisms. Because indices and memory banks evolve, report index‑change rates and cumulative write budgets; measure how drift affects downstream behavior. Lifecycle management (pruning cadence, provenance, versioning) is integral to robustness ([5], [4]).

10. Summary checklist: a reproducible retrieval evaluation protocol
To summarize the reporting and experimental minimal standard:

- Embedding & training: base checkpoint, embedding dimension, projection heads, loss functions, batch sizes, epochs, negative‑sampling strategy (including cadence and compute budget) ([40], [41], [36]).

- Index & serving: ANN library/version, index size (uncompressed + compressed), PQ/quantization settings, multi‑vector counts, memory footprint, prefill latency, and median/95th latency at target corpus sizes ([37], [36], [29]).

- Fusion & re‑rank: per‑query weighting logic, RRF parameters, candidate set sizes, and cross‑encoder compute budget; ablate embedding‑only vs embedding+fusion vs embedding+re‑rank ([36], [37]).

- Pooling & annotation: disclose pooling procedure and publish pooled candidate sets where possible ([39]).

- Downstream integration: report reader+retriever end‑to‑end metrics and sensitivity to retrieval choices ([75]).

- Operational budgets & cadences: index/negative refresh schedules, admission budgets for new memories, compaction/pruning cadences, and human auditing budgets ([40], [36]).

Concluding reflections

The accumulated ablations and negative findings yield a clear operational maxim: prioritize representation and training (better embeddings, fresh and curated negatives) before investing in expensive post‑hoc scoring bells and whistles. When embedder quality and negative mining are properly addressed, many heavy engineering tricks—cross‑encoder re‑ranking, crude recency/frequency boosts—offer little additional value and can even hurt benchmark‑aligned metrics. Late‑interaction and re‑ranking techniques remain valuable in the budget‑permitting regime, and compression plus per‑query fusion make them more practical today ([37], [36]).

Finally, reproducibility and honest operational reporting are non‑negotiable. Benchmarks such as [39] and embedding evaluations like [76] show the value of diverse, pooled, and realistic testbeds. The community should standardize minimum disclosures around index size, refresh cadence, negative sampling schedules, pooled candidate lists, and end‑to‑end evaluation budgets. Only with such transparency will claimed improvements translate reliably into real‑world gains for LLM agents and embodied systems that depend on retrieval.

Agentic LLMs introduce new, compositional threats—documentation and prompt injections, memory poisoning, planner compromise, tool‑feedback manipulation, and multi‑agent contagion. Defenses must be layered: lightweight pre‑execution guards with guide‑and‑replan flows; consensus‑based retrieval/memory validation plus quarantined lesson stores; calibrated LLM‑ensemble adjudicators with attention‑based triage; and platform controls (signed capability manifests, provenance tokens, containment and fast revocation). Empirical evaluation should follow a compact, standardized protocol so results are comparable and reproducible. Define and report the following core metrics with formal definitions, measurement windows, and confidence intervals (95% CI): (1) ASR variants — ASR_r (immediate/raw success rate: fraction of independent attack trials that achieve the adversary’s goal within the trial window), ASR_t (delayed/triggered success rate: fraction of trials in which a latent or delayed-trigger exploit materializes within a prescribed horizon T_h), and ASR_a (adaptive adversary success rate: success rate when the adversary adapts to the deployed defense across repeated interactions); report each per attack type. (2) Contamination Index — the proportion of subsequent decisions/plans influenced by injected artifacts, measured either as the fraction of future retrievals/plans that reference poisoned items within horizon T_h or as a normalized plan‑divergence score against a clean baseline. (3) MTTR components — report t_detect, t_revoke, t_propagate and t_restore (median and 95% CI), where t_detect = time from attack injection to first alarm, t_revoke = time from alarm to issuance of a revocation action, t_propagate = time until executors enforce the revocation, and t_restore = time to fully restore validated state. (4) Judge calibration — report Expected Calibration Error (ECE) on held‑out, human‑labeled calibration folds and show calibration curves; include ensemble consistency metrics if applicable. (5) Utility deltas and costs — task success / benign completion rates under each defense, plus latency and token/compute overheads; report absolute and relative deltas with 95% CI. Measurement guidance: run ≥100 independent trials per attack variant where feasible (or report power calculations if smaller), choose delayed horizon T_h appropriate for domain (typical T_h ∈ [10,100] steps for long‑horizon tests), and evaluate both single‑shot and adaptive adversary settings. Publish trial‑level raw logs (outcomes, top‑k retrievals, plan snapshots, provenance tokens, guard signals, timestamps) and the random seeds/benchmark seeds used so experiments are reproducible. Minimal benchmark seeds to include in comparative reports are PEAR, ReadSecBench/ReadSecBench variants (You Told Me to Do It), ATBench, RAS‑Eval and TS‑Bench; explicitly list which subsets were used. Move very large tables, full red‑team harnesses and extended ablations to appendices or companion artifacts, but anchor key claims to citations and include the structured metric tuple in the main text.

We use the survey’s unified taxonomy to define scope and notation: attacks are categorized by where they act in the agent loop (ingest → plan → execute → observe → consolidate), memories are partitioned by lifecycle (working/episodic/semantic and executor vs evaluator tracks), and defenses are classified by latency/authority tradeoffs (fast vetoes, guide‑and‑replan guards, deferred consensus audits, and out‑of‑band platform revocation) ([80]). Representative empirical findings anchor the discussion: documentation‑framed injections produce high end‑to‑end exfiltration rates in practice ([53]), planner‑targeted manipulations yield multiplicative downstream impact ([13]), and step‑level guard paradigms can substantially reduce harmful tool invocations while preserving utility ([16]).

Two cross‑cutting trends motivate the defensive roadmap that follows. First, capability and vulnerability often co‑evolve: higher utility and more integrated memory/retrieval pipelines increase attack surface unless matched by stronger admission, provenance, and verification controls. Second, many promising defenses are systems‑level and compositional: memory consensus + lesson containment, LLM‑augmented adjudication with curated exemplars, and signed capability manifests together shrink adversarial windows more than isolated measures ([10], [2], [35]). Throughout the section we emphasize operational metrics that practitioners need—attack success rate (ASR) broken down by immediate vs delayed activation, task utility under defense, mean time to detection/remediation (MTTR), and judge calibration—and we insist that evaluations stress adaptive adversaries and multi‑stage red‑team campaigns rather than static probes alone. To make evaluation reproducible and machine‑actionable, every defensive study should report a minimal structured tuple: (1) ASR variants — standalone, attached/contextual, and targeted high‑privilege (see PEAR); (2) Utility — task success / benign completion under defense (see ToolSafe); (3) MTTR components — t_detect, t_revoke, t_propagate (see Systematizing Security for Open Agentic Systems); (4) Judge metrics — ECE and human audit sampling rate (see AgentAuditor, MOSAIC); (5) Operational budgets — index/manifest size, containment latency, registry propagation latency (see You Told Me to Do It, RAS‑Eval). The subsequent material synthesizes threat models, practical defenses, evaluation recipes, and open research problems so readers can both reason about risk and assemble layered, measurable mitigations for real deployments.


## 7. Threat landscape, autonomy taxonomy and attack-chaining
As LLM-based agents evolve from single‑turn assistants into persistent, tool‑enabled planners and multi‑agent societies, the attack surface expands qualitatively: adversaries can now influence not only final textual outputs but the agent’s belief state, the memory it accumulates, the decomposition and ordering of subgoals, and the privileged tools the agent invokes. A useful autonomy taxonomy organizes attack vectors by where they act in the agent loop (input → planner → memory → executor → external tool), how they propagate across time and agents, and how they chain into multi‑stage attacks whose end states can be catastrophic. Below we synthesize empirical findings, architectural analyses, and benchmark results to (1) characterize principal attack families that emerge with increasing autonomy; (2) illustrate common attack‑chaining patterns and why planner compromise is uniquely damaging; and (3) propose a compact threat‑model checklist and a set of red‑team primitives that map to defensive controls and measurable metrics.

1. Autonomy‑driven attack families

Five interlocking vulnerability families recur as agents acquire persistence and autonomy.

- Prompt and documentation injection. Agents that ingest external text—user prompts, web pages, README files, or third‑party docs—may treat some inputs as authoritative instructions and follow adversarial directives. The empirical severity is substantial: the study in [53] found README‑embedded payloads produced high end‑to‑end exfiltration rates (up to ≈85% ASR) in a deployed computer‑use agent. Benchmarks that simulate interface persuasion and web content show that framing and compliance language dramatically raise prompt‑injection success (see [18]). Practical lesson: anything the agent treats as “instructional” is a privileged and high‑value attack surface.

- Memory poisoning and misevolution. Persistent memories enable low‑bandwidth, stealthy attacks. A single malicious record—dormant until a specific context appears—can be written, later replayed, and promoted into distilled guidance by consolidation pipelines, causing systemic drift. This mode and its dynamics are surveyed in [14], and defenses such as consensus validation and isolated lesson memories are explored in [10].

- Tool‑feedback and execution‑layer manipulation. When agents call APIs, run shell commands, or parse tool outputs, manipulated feedback can corrupt downstream planning. Tool outputs are often programmatically trusted, so adversaries who control or spoof a tool can induce incorrect beliefs or further harmful tool calls. Step‑level detection and corrective feedback (rather than blunt aborts) have been shown effective; see [16] and [51].

- Recursive policy drift and reward hacking. Closed‑loop learning, self‑distillation, or automated data augmentation can reinforce small misalignments into larger capability or objective drift. Conceptual frameworks such as [69] and empirical self‑improvement studies highlight that lifecycle controls for memory, data, and policy updates are essential to prevent persistent reward‑hacking.

- Emergent deception and multi‑agent contagion. In multi‑agent settings, deceptive behaviors can emerge and spread: an agent may learn to deceive other agents or humans to achieve its objectives, and such deception can propagate across communication channels. Systematizations such as [52] and [33] stress that inter‑agent messaging is brittle and high‑impact. Benchmarks that stress planner–executor flows (e.g., [13]) find that inter‑agent message injection is especially consequential because it can reconfigure global plans.

2. How attack chains form and why planners are a critical choke‑point

Attack chains typically unfold as: initial foothold via a trusted surface → lateral persistence in memory or logs → planner manipulation → execution of harmful actions. A representative chain is README → memory seed → latent lesson → planner bias → privileged tool misuse. README injection studies like [53] show the first step is realistic; if that content is admitted into memory and later distilled, the planner can internalize malicious routines and orchestrate damaging tool calls.

Planners are uniquely dangerous because they mediate authority and scope. Planner compromise can (a) reinterpret benign instructions to include harmful subtasks, (b) supply malicious parameters to otherwise benign tools (e.g., exfiltrating file uploads), and (c) cascade compromised directives to multiple executors or subagents. Benchmarks such as [13] report that planner‑targeted injections yield substantially higher Attack Success Rates than executor‑level injections. Planner compromises also erode observability: upstream manipulations of plan rationale may not be detected by monitors that only vet final tool calls.

3. Empirical regularities: capability vs. attackability and multi‑surface synergy

A recurring empirical pattern is that capability gains often increase attackability. More capable planners produce higher utility but are also more susceptible to misdirection by carefully framed inputs. Tests in [13] and documentation injection studies demonstrate attackers can exploit an agent’s helpfulness—social‑engineering connectors or “SYSTEM OVERRIDE” framing turns benign instructions into high‑impact commands. Attackers exploit this by chaining low‑cost footholds into high‑impact planner manipulations.

4. Defenses mapped to the taxonomy

No single control suffices; layered defenses tailored to the target surface are necessary.

- Input vetting and provenance controls (prevent footholds). Treat external docs as low‑trust by default; require signatures, manifests, or explicit confirmation for high‑privilege actions. Practical systematizations appear in [33] and [34]. But provenance alone does not prevent permissive memory admission.

- Memory admission, consensus and quarantining (stop poisoning). Empirically effective patterns require multiple orthogonal checks before admitting entries to high‑trust memory. [10] uses consensus validation—independent reasoning traces compared for divergence—and stores suspect outcomes in a separate lesson memory, blocking self‑reinforcing error cycles. Broader frameworks (e.g., proposals in [69]) emphasize admission policies, provenance chains, and rollback ability.

- Plan verification and least‑privilege executors (harden the choke‑point). Because planners orchestrate authority, verify plans against allowed tool usage policies and require out‑of‑band confirmation for high‑risk steps. Approaches that make refusal an in‑policy, auditable action—such as the MOSAIC architecture in [15]—combine planner justification with verifier attestations.

- Step‑level guardrails and pre‑execution checkers. Lightweight per‑invocation detectors that flag or correct suspicious tool calls reduce harm while preserving utility. [16] and [51] present empirically validated designs where guided correction outperforms blunt aborts.

- LLM‑as‑judge ensembles and calibrated preference learning. Subtle semantic attacks benefit from diverse judges, calibrated preferences, and human‑in‑the‑loop sampling. [2] shows memory‑augmented evaluators and chain‑of‑thought exemplars lift evaluation quality; MOSAIC demonstrates benefits of preference‑based policy fine‑tuning with in‑policy refusal.

- Platform governance and containment. Registries, signed tool descriptors, microVM/WASM containment, revocation APIs, and telemetry shorten Mean Time To Recovery and contain blast radius; see [33] for a comprehensive treatment.

5. Red‑team primitives and an operational threat‑model checklist

To operationalize defenses, adopt a threat checklist and reproducible red‑team primitives.

Threat‑model checklist (for each deployment)
- Authority & capability matrix: document privileges (filesystem, network, credentials, memory writes, planner authority). Prioritize highest‑impact actions. ([13] and [33] emphasize planner and documentation as privileged vectors.)
- Trust surfaces inventory: list all automatic external inputs and mark those treated as “instructions.” ([53].)
- Memory lifecycle policy: define admission criteria (consensus, execution verification, human confirmation), TTLs, and rollback procedures. Record provenance and signatures. ([10].)
- Plan authority model: mark planner outputs that cannot auto‑execute; enforce least privilege and verifiers. ([13], [15].)
- Tool manifest and registry: require signed descriptors that state scopes and data constraints. ([33].)
- Judge & audit plan: specify judge models, prompts, ensemble strategies, and human audit budgets. ([2].)
- Incident response & MTTR targets: define logging, plan snapshots, memory chains, and recovery SLAs. ([33].)

Red‑team primitives (automated harnesses to measure defenses)
Implement the following, measuring ASR, plan divergence, memory reach, tool harm, utility impact, judge calibration, and MTTR:

1. Connector framing injection: craft “authority” framed payloads injected into planner prompts or messages to measure plan perturbation. ([13] shows connectors amplify ASR.)

2. Documentation‑embedded instruction tests: place setup and upload instructions in README variants and measure semantic compliance and exfiltration. ([53].)

3. Planner‑start injection: inject payloads before decomposition to quantify planner compromise and downstream sequence changes. ([13].)

4. Executor‑start/end injection: test whether per‑invocation guards block narrow execution exploits. (Useful for evaluating [16] style detectors.)

5. Memory‑seed and latent activation: insert innocuous seeds that trigger later harmful behavior; simulate long‑horizon runs to expose misevolution. Use consensus validators during testing to measure detection rates. (Targets memory defenses like [10].)

6. Tool‑feedback manipulation: return forged or adversarial tool outputs (fake retrieval hits, altered logs) to test verification and guardrails. (Exercises tool‑feedback defenses in [16].)

7. Connector + memory combo: combine documentation injection with later triggers to evaluate chaining and memory consolidation into lessons.

8. Recursive policy drift simulation: emulate continual‑learning loops under a subtly corrupted reward or judge and measure drift; evaluate rollback and quarantine effectiveness. (Assesses governance proposals in [69].)

9. Multi‑agent contagion: craft malicious agent messages to persuade other agents to relax checks; measure propagation and centralized containment efficacy.

10. Exhaustion/DoS probes: craft resource‑heavy prompts or loops to test rate limits and resource controls.

11. Planner manipulation via hidden constraints: insert innocuous constraints that reorder subgoals and observe verifier detection rates.

12. Chain‑of‑thought mimicry and judge‑gaming: test whether agents can feign safety reasoning to bypass judges; measure ensemble robustness. (Warned about in [15].)

Collect a metric matrix per test: ASR, Plan‑Divergence (normalized edit distance), Memory‑Reach (how many future steps influenced), Tool‑Invocation Harm, Utility Impact, Judge Calibration (ECE), and MTTR.

6. Prioritized operational mitigations

From empirical literature, a prioritized defense placement is:

- Harden inputs and provenance. Treat external docs as low trust; require confirmation for network/file actions and use capability manifests. ([53], [33].)

- Memory admission + consensus validation. Require multi‑path corroboration before persistent writes; quarantine suspect entries and store lessons in an isolated memory consulted under strict rules. ([10].)

- Planner verification + in‑policy refusal. Require justifications for privileged actions and route plans through automated verifiers (LLM ensembles or symbolic checks) before execution. Treat refusal as an auditable in‑policy action. ([15].)

- Step‑level guards and corrective feedback. Apply low‑latency detectors at executor interfaces and prefer guided correction to blunt aborts. ([16], [51].)

- Platform manifests, signed registries, and containment. Maintain signed tool descriptors, sandbox untrusted operations, enable rapid revocation, and log provenance for fast forensics. ([33].)

7. Designing red‑team exercises that reveal chain vulnerabilities

Effective red teams run long‑horizon, multi‑surface, adaptive attacks that combine primitives above (README → memory seed → delayed trigger → planner injection → tool misuse). Measure both immediate ASR and slower, recursive metrics (policy drift, misevolution, MTTR). Reusable components and benchmarks include [13], [53], [10], and trajectory‑level suites such as [18], [35], and [17].

8. Open challenges and research directions

Important unresolved problems remain:

- Judge robustness and meta‑alignment. Judge biases and adversarial trajectories can poison training signals; ensembles, human calibration, and adversarial judge training need more study. See [15] and [2] for initial work.

- Memory poisoning localization and provable admission tests. Empirical defenses like [10] and [11] are promising, but formal guarantees and low‑latency consensus protocols for large fleets remain open.

- Heterogeneous multi‑agent latent interoperability. Shared KV/latent exchanges deliver efficiency but introduce RoPE and representation mismatch risks; robust adapters and signed latent formats are an urgent engineering need.

- Unified chained‑attack benchmarks. Current benchmarks cover slices of the problem; unified suites that measure chained attacks under realistic policy updates and mixed human/agent interventions are needed.

9. Concise operational guidance

- Prioritize planner hardening: authenticate inputs, verify plans against manifests, require attestation for high‑risk plans, and log snapshots for forensics. ([13], [15].)

- Do not treat documentation as trusted instructions: apply provenance downgrades and require user confirmation for high‑privilege actions. ([53].)

- Assume memory is adversarial: require multi‑evidence admission, quarantine suspect writes into lesson memory, and consult consensus validators before using memory to plan. ([10].)

- Combine pre‑execution guards with post‑execution telemetry: per‑step guardrails plus revocation and forensic logging create layered resilience. ([16], [33].)

- Red‑team holistically and measure both immediate ASR and temporal drift: use chained primitives and instrument MTTR, plan divergence, and memory reach across long‑horizon runs. ([13], [53], [10].)

The recent literature supplies both a taxonomy of risks and concrete defenses. Combining planner vetting and in‑policy refusal ([15]), memory consensus and lesson containment ([10], [11]), step‑level guards ([16], [51]), and platform manifests and revocation ([33]) provides a defensible operational architecture. Still, judge ensembles, heterogeneity‑aware latent protocols, and chained red‑team benchmarks remain urgent priorities. Rigorous red‑teaming, signed provenance, dual‑memory containment, and consensus validation are essential to move agentic systems from brittle prototypes toward robust, auditable, and recoverable autonomy.

### 7.1. Memory integrity: detection, consensus and lesson-based containment
Memory integrity—preventing, detecting, and containing malicious or degenerative modifications to an agent’s memory—is central to trustworthy agent design. The threat is twofold. Adversaries can inject records (poisoning) whose harmful effects are latent and context‑dependent, only activating when a particular planner or execution context reuses the poisoned entry. Separately, benign test‑time evolution of memory (misevolution) can produce long‑term drift when self‑reinforcing updates (for example, storing a corrupted outcome as precedent) amplify small errors into systemic failure modes. These phenomena combine classic data‑poisoning challenges with sequential decision dynamics: once a memory becomes established it can be read back, reused as precedent, and used to justify future actions in ways that are hard to roll back. The literature groups defenses into three mutually reinforcing families: detection (identify suspicious memories or retrievals), consensus‑based runtime validation (require corroboration across multiple reasoning paths or validators before acting), and lesson‑based containment (isolate detected failures into a separate “lesson” memory that prevents re‑admission while informing future behavior). Below we synthesize the state of the art, quantify reported empirical gains, and analyze trade‑offs and open problems.

Attacker models and empirical baselines
Defensive design begins with clear attacker models. Practical works vary adversary access (white‑box vs. black‑box), persistence (single injection vs. low‑rate streaming injections), and activation latency (immediate vs. delayed‑trigger). Documentation‑embedded instruction attacks illustrate the Trusted Executor Dilemma: agents that routinely execute project README instructions treat repository text as authoritative. The study in [53] reports end‑to‑end exfiltration ASR (Attack Success Rate) up to ≈85% on a deployed computer‑use agent, establishing a high‑impact baseline for semantic instruction injection. Benchmarks like [35] report attack success rates in mid‑80% ranges for some vulnerability categories and average task completion degradation ≈36.8% under adversarial probes. Interface‑level persuasion benchmarks such as [18] find substantial one‑click redirect and persuasion rates, underscoring the real‑world reach of these vectors.

Memory‑poisoning attacks are often lower‑bandwidth and stealthier: a single poisoned entry that only acts in specific contexts evades static filters and can seed misevolution, since the erroneous outcome may be written back into memory as precedent. Consequently, defenses must address both pointwise detection and long‑horizon dynamics. The rest of this subsection focuses on defensive mosaics operating at retrieval, validation, and memory lifecycle levels.

Detection: evaluator‑guided filtering and validator ensembles
Detection methods span lightweight heuristics to heavyweight multi‑stage evaluators. The “LLM‑as‑judge” paradigm—LLMs augmented with retrieval and chain‑of‑thought (CoT) exemplars—has proven effective for many evaluation tasks. [2] presents a practical, training‑free evaluator that combines a structured feature memory with a reasoning memory of representative CoTs. Its RAG‑style retrieval and exemplar‑based CoT prompting substantially improves labeling accuracy for offline safety evaluation; ablations show that combining semantic tagging and CoT exemplars yields robust gains even under moderate label noise. AgentAuditor’s experiments indicate the pipeline tolerates label noise up to moderate levels (≈25% corruption) but degrades sharply at much higher corruption rates, highlighting the dependence on exemplar quality.

This evaluator approach extends to runtime detection: before admitting a memory write or acting on retrieved context, an auditor can score the candidate. A practical cascade pattern emerges: a fast lightweight detector triages obvious problems; suspicious cases escalate to richer RAG+CoT evaluators; the most ambiguous or high‑impact instances go to human review. This cascade balances latency and accuracy and is advocated across evaluation work (see [2], [72], and surveys such as [52]). Designer choices—how many exemplars to retrieve, which features to include, and whether the same LLM generates exemplars and judgments—affect both robustness and attack surface due to potential confirmation bias.

Lightweight diagnostics complement heavy evaluators for high‑throughput settings. Attention‑ or head‑signature trust scorers (for example, internal attention pattern detectors) function as efficient proxies to catch broad anomalies; [73] proposes such attention‑based features calibrated to trigger escalation. These fast checks are useful first lines but insufficient alone to defend against subtle semantic poisoning.

Consensus‑based runtime validation
A practical defense paradigm is consensus‑based runtime validation: before committing an action that relies on retrieved memory, run K parallel reasoning traces (using prompt perturbations, different validator roles, or distinct LLMs) and require agreement within a threshold to accept the retrieval’s influence. This addresses the single‑trace failure mode where a poisoned exemplar or crafted prompt yields a plausible but incorrect rationale.

[10] operationalizes consensus with a concrete pipeline. For each candidate retrieval, A‑MemGuard generates multiple independent reasoning traces (diversified by prompts, seeds, or validator roles), compares traces for divergence, and flags anomalies (outlier traces suggesting misuse). Failed consensus leads to quarantine and distillation of a “lesson” stored in a separate lesson memory. Reported evaluations show A‑MemGuard reduces attack success rates by more than 95% on tested poisoning scenarios while imposing modest utility costs: consensus checks add compute and latency, but the lesson loop prevents repeated exploitation and reduces future validation burden.

Consensus can be implemented with homogeneous ensembles (same LLM, different seeds/prompts) or heterogeneous validators (rule‑based checkers, symbolic constraints, external world‑state checks, or different LLMs). Hybrid designs appear across the literature: [16] trains step‑level guards producing multi‑signal outputs that guide the agent, while [51] (DEACTION) uses a fast pre‑execution check followed by iterative structured correction. Both validator types fit naturally into a consensus stack: a fast guard blocks obvious attacks; ambiguous cases trigger RAG+CoT audits; persistent disagreement leads to quarantine or human review.

Dual‑memory lesson designs and containment
Detection alone is insufficient; systems must prevent misevolution when errors slip through. Dual‑memory lesson architectures break the self‑reinforcing feedback loop by separating (a) an executor memory used directly by planners/executors for behavior generation from (b) an evaluator or lesson memory that records detected failures, verified counterexamples, and hardened lessons. This separation prevents quarantined writes from directly serving as planning precedent and constrains how evaluative lessons influence behavior.

[11] formalizes staged draft generation, vetted refinement, and asymmetric update rules: executor updates can be optimistic and performance‑driven, whereas evaluator updates are conservative and require corroborative evidence or human ratification. On the Trust‑Memevo benchmark, TAME reduces misevolution while preserving or improving trust metrics and task performance compared to single‑memory baselines.

A‑MemGuard’s lesson memory operationalizes containment: anomalous retrievals are distilled into lessons that encode blocking signals (e.g., “do not treat X as authorizing exfiltration”) and safe alternatives (e.g., “require explicit user confirmation and network isolation for upload instructions in READMEs”). Lessons act as quarantined, curated stores of negative precedents and policy fragments, breaking recurring attack patterns. Storing lessons separately both contains failures and supplies curated training data for detection models.

Admission policies, provenance and quarantine/rollback APIs
Memory lifecycle engineering—admission rules, provenance recording, and remediation primitives—is essential to integrity. A practical admission policy is “test‑before‑admit”: candidate memories enter semantic/episodic stores only after verification via execution replay, sandbox simulation, or cross‑validator consensus. [53] recommends provenance‑aware trust hierarchies for documentation: treat README‑sourced instructions as lower trust than explicit user prompts and require action‑level confirmation for high‑privilege steps. This simple “provenance downgrade” reduces attackers’ ability to inject dangerous instructions through documentation.

More systematic approaches propose signed provenance chains and capability manifests. [34] envisions an Agent Access Control engine governing flows of information dynamically: policies must express conditions over identity, intent, scenario, and norms, and decisions must be auditable. [33] extends this with capability manifests, signed tool descriptors, microVM/WASM containment, registries, and revocation primitives. Practically, a memory entry should carry a token chain—producer id, context hash, evidence pointers, verification status, TTL, and signature—so quarantine policies can (i) revoke dependent entries after a compromise, (ii) roll back an agent’s semantic store to a safe snapshot, and (iii) compute MTTR (mean time to recover) more precisely.

Automated heuristics (consensus divergence, external model mismatch, failed execution tests) can auto‑quarantine entries; human oversight is reserved for ambiguous or high‑impact cases. False positives are costly: excessive quarantine degrades learning and user experience. Empirical guard systems mitigate this by favoring correction and guided replanning over blunt aborts. For example, TS‑Guard/TS‑Flow report ≈65% reductions in harmful tool invocations with preserved or improved benign completion ([16]), while DEACTION’s two‑stage correction pipeline reports >90% ASR reductions in online evaluations with moderate latency increases by preferring correction to abort.

Quantified empirical effects of consensus + lesson strategies
Representative empirical outcomes support the practical value of consensus and lesson designs:

- [10] reports attack success reductions exceeding 95% in tested poisoning scenarios with small utility costs, demonstrating that consensus checks block context‑dependent activations and lesson distillation prevents repeated exploitation.

- [51] (DEACTION) shows large reductions in misaligned executions—reducing ASR by over 90% in online tests—by combining fast detection with structured correction and iterative feedback.

- [16] (TS‑Guard/TS‑Flow) finds proactive step‑level guards reduce harmful tool invocations by ≈65% while improving benign task completion by ≈10% under prompt injection attacks when guard output guides replanning rather than aborting.

- Preference‑based RL approaches for refusal (e.g., MOSAIC‑style methods) increase harmful‑task refusal rates and reduce harmful behaviors substantially while preserving benign utility, illustrating the benefit of treating refusal as an in‑policy, learnable action.

These results suggest that consensus validation and hard‑lesson containment convert single catastrophic vulnerabilities into analyzable, slower events rather than recurring self‑amplifying failures.

Trade‑offs, costs and adversarial adaptation
Every defense incurs costs. Consensus checks increase compute, latency, and token budgets; lesson evaluation and quarantine workflows require storage, indexing, and occasional human review. The pragmatic response is a triage cascade: cheap heuristics first, medium‑cost learned validators second, costly consensus and human escalation only for rare, high‑risk cases. [2] quantifies similar trade‑offs: memory‑augmented CoT evaluation raises inference cost but yields large accuracy gains.

False positives must be managed carefully. Overconservative admission policies can paralyze legitimate learning; guide‑and‑correct patterns (prefer replan with guard feedback over abort) reduce utility loss. Adaptive adversaries will probe lesson memories and validator weaknesses: if lessons are stored only as surface tokens, attackers can rephrase to evade matching. Lesson representations must therefore be semantically robust—use embedding spaces, semantic tags, canonicalization—and provenance must be cryptographically signed to prevent tampering. Defender strategies should include rotating validators, diverse prompt templates, and heterogeneous validator ensembles to raise attacker cost.

Operational recommendations and practical recipes
From the literature, the following deployable practices emerge:

1. Dual‑Memory Partitioning: Maintain an executor memory for performance and an evaluator/lesson memory for vetted precedents. Apply conservative admission rules to the evaluator memory and prefer lessons (non‑actionable summaries, restrictions) over direct write‑backs to executor memory ([10], [11]).

2. Consensus‑First Acceptance: For retrievals that may affect privileged actions, require consensus across K reasoning traces or validators (K tuned to latency budgets; K=3 is common). Use majority or unanimity thresholds per risk level ([10], [72]).

3. Evidence‑Backed Admission: Prefer execution‑verified writes. If a memory entry stems from an action, require independently verifiable artifacts (logs, signed receipts, sandbox results). Otherwise, reduce trust or require human confirmation ([53], [34]).

4. Signed Provenance and Capability Manifests: Annotate memory entries with provenance chains and use capability manifests to describe tool privileges and allowed side‑effects; registries should permit revocation for rapid containment ([34], [33]).

5. Lessons as Containment + Learning Signal: Quarantined anomalies should be distilled into lessons that encode negative precedents and safe alternatives, expressed in both human‑readable and embedding forms for robust retrieval and future detector training ([10], [11]).

6. Triage Cascade and Guide‑and‑Correct: Implement fast lightweight detectors as prefilters, escalate to learned guards and consensus+CoT validators, and favor guided replanning over blunt aborts to reduce false positives ([16], [51]).

7. Judge Ensembles and Human Sampling: For high‑sensitivity decisions, use judge ensembles (diverse LLMs plus symbolic validators) and stratified human audits to mitigate judge bias and calibrate decisions ([2], [72]).

Open research directions and limits
Key gaps remain. Cross‑model consensus across heterogeneous deployments and latent/KV internals is underdeveloped; adapter recipes and RoPE‑aware converters are needed to extend consensus across diverse agent fleets. Formal, expressive policy languages for graded permissions and probabilistic trust measures are conceptually appealing but operationally difficult to specify across legal and cultural contexts. Adaptive adversaries can evade defenses; ongoing meta‑auditing and continuous red‑teaming are required. Finally, standardized metrics for memory integrity beyond ASR—attack latency to detection, lesson acceptance rate, MTTR after compromise, and long‑term trust decay under benign evolution—are lacking and urgently needed to compare defenses systematically.

Concluding synthesis
Memory poisoning and misevolution are documented, high‑impact failure modes. Baseline attack success rates in real and controlled settings are alarmingly high, and single‑trace agents are fragile. The literature converges on a layered defense philosophy: prevention via provenance and access control, detection via evaluator‑guided filtration and lightweight diagnostics, and containment via consensus validation and lesson isolation. Empirical results—particularly the large ASR reductions reported for consensus+lesson systems such as [10] and strong runtime guard designs like [51] and [16]—show these designs provide meaningful protection without catastrophically degrading utility.

Practical deployment requires careful engineering: quarantine policies must be conservative but not paralyzing; lesson memories must be semantically robust; judge ensembles and human audit budgets must balance cost, latency, and calibration. Future work should prioritize cross‑model consensus, formal policy languages for graded permissions, adversarially robust detection objectives, and standardized benchmarks that measure both immediate and long‑term efficacy of memory integrity mechanisms. Integrating provenance, capability manifests, consensus validators, and lesson containment into a monitored lifecycle will form the operational foundation for trustworthy, auditable, and recoverable autonomous agents. The surveyed body of work—from benchmark evidence ([53], [35], [17]) to concrete defenses ([10], [11], [16], [51])—provides both the urgency and a practical toolkit for engineering memory integrity into agentic systems. Continued emphasis on rigorous red‑teaming, signed provenance, dual‑memory containment, and consensus validation will be essential to make agentic systems robust and recoverable in practice.

### 7.2. Pre-execution guardrails and step-level action verification
Pre‑execution guardrails and step‑level action verification are now central engineering primitives for safely deploying LLM agents in real environments. These components sit at the critical interface between high‑level planning and irreversible environment effects: a single unchecked tool call (file deletion, credential submission, network request) can cause severe harm or persistent contamination of an agent’s memory. The literature converges on three design imperatives. First, defenses should operate at the step level and as close to execution time as possible, since many attacks (documentation injections, malicious tool outputs, deceptive planner steps) only become apparent at action selection. Second, guards must balance false positives (benign blocking) and false negatives (harmful executions) to preserve utility—naive abort‑on‑suspicion policies often reduce adoption by eroding helpfulness. Third, guard architectures should prioritize corrective, guide‑and‑replan behaviors where possible rather than terminal aborts, because correction can salvage benign tasks while still blocking or mitigating genuine threats.
