3) If specialized latents are desired, adopt a staged curriculum: CoT → SFT latent alignment → RL invocation refinement. Concrete exemplars include [8] and [45], which structure such curricula and show empirical effectiveness.

4) Use weak orthogonality and sparsity penalties, but keep them small and validate base‑LM performance. If orthogonality is necessary, prefer adapter training (LoRA) rather than blanket full‑model penalties to shield base modeling performance;  demonstrates this trade‑off.

5) For heterogeneous multi‑model assemblages, prefer learned routing and per‑layer projectors ([46]) or small trained converters rather than raw KV concatenation. Train routers with load‑balance and entropy penalties and reward them on downstream utility.

6) When latent invocation is costly or potentially harmful, use a trained trigger (RL‑based) as in [12] so latents are synthesized only when expected utility exceeds invocation cost. Train the weaver with SFT, then refine with RL; regularize with KL to the teacher policy to stabilize.

7) Protect safety‑critical judgments by decoupling latent extractors and reasoners and training only lightweight adapters ([50]). Maintain signed textual provenance and constrained admission rules for memories (e.g., requirement of execution verification for stored lessons, see [26] and [25]).

8) Evaluate under heterogeneity and offset perturbations. Before deployment run RoPE offset and anchor‑pool stress tests (as in [29]) and heterogeneity mixing to check for brittle failure modes.

Open research directions and unresolved tensions
Several cross‑cutting open problems remain:

- Balancing interpretability and specialization. How can we make latent channels auditable without nullifying their compression advantage? Hybrid protocols that produce small textual checkpoints for human audit alongside latents are an initial engineering approach ([1], [12]), but principled approaches (e.g., latent→text probes with fidelity guarantees) are scarce.

- Heterogeneous‑agent latent interoperability. Adapter recipes for latent exchange remain nascent—[1] articulates the need for per‑layer converters and [29] proposes anchor‑based heuristics—but an agreed canonical approach with formal fidelity guarantees is not yet established.

- Robust latent evaluation. Existing diagnostics are useful but ad hoc; a reproducible benchmark suite that stresses specialization under heterogeneity, adversarial latents and memory misevolution would accelerate progress. Promising design elements are laid out across several papers ([1], [29], [50]), but a unifying benchmark remains to be built.

- Regularizer design that is both effective and safe. Orthogonality and sparsity help, but they can also reduce base‑LM performance; designing regularizers that target functional redundancy (affecting downstream behavior) rather than raw representational orthogonality is a subtle, open challenge. Mixed objectives and proxy functions (e.g., downstream‑task‑aware orthogonality penalties, contrastive alignments with task‑reward kernels) show promise in preliminary ablations (, [47]).

Conclusion: trade‑offs and guiding principles
The surveyed work suggests a clear set of guiding principles for inducing latent specialization:

- Provide explicit incentives aligned with final task utility (teacher–student SFT + RL), rather than relying on incidental LM‑likelihood gradients alone. Teacher–student pipelines such as those in [8] and [45], followed by conservative policy updates (VLPO/Flow‑GRPO), are effective.

- Architecturally enforce opportunities for specialization (router/gating, primitives) so modules have functional niches to grow into ([46], [6]). Without structural partitioning, gradients favor redundant reuse.

- Regularize, but gently and with validation against base‑LM metrics. Orthogonality, sparsity and contrastive terms are powerful, but strong penalties can detach latent modules from the decoder’s expected input distribution and thus harm decoding performance ().

- Start with training‑free, conservative protocols when operational constraints demand it (homogeneous backbones, rapid deployment). Then progressively add learned components (adapters, routers, triggers) guided by curriculum and robust evaluators ([1], [29], [50]).

- Do not forget safety and auditability. Latent specialization increases efficiency but reduces transparency; adopt hybrid designs that preserve readable checkpoints (textual lessons, signed provenance) and strong admission filters for memories (see [26], [25], [50]).

Across the surveyed literature—from training‑free KV concatenation to learned coprocessors, teacher–student distillation and RL‑augmented triggers—the central lesson is stable: specialization emerges only when the system’s objectives, architecture and training schedule align to reward distinct, task‑relevant computation. The most promising systems therefore combine multiple mechanisms—staged curricula, weak structural regularizers, routing/primitive scaffolds and conservative RL or adapter‑based fine‑tuning—to induce specialization while preserving base‑model competence and auditability. Concrete evidence and complementary strategies can be found in [1], [47], [46], [3], , [8], [45], [12], [6], [29], [50], [48], [25], [62], and [26]. These works supply practical mechanisms that practitioners can mix and match depending on constraints (homogeneity, safety criticality, available compute) to achieve robust latent specialization in practice.

### 10.5. Evaluation & benchmark alignment: avoiding metric mismatches
Evaluation and benchmark alignment is one of the most consequential—and under-specified—pieces of research infrastructure for LLM‑based agentic systems. Rapid methodological innovation has produced many new architectures (latent channels, KV‑cache reuse, multi‑agent orchestration), memory regimes (episodic KGs, generative latent memories, self‑evolving playbooks), and safety harnesses (pre‑execution guards, runtime auditors, meta‑audit enumerators). Small but substantive differences in experimental scaffolding can dominate reported gains: differing index sizes, refresh cadences, hardware stacks, judge prompts, or pooling procedures all shift the measurement baseline. Without careful reporting and controlled comparisons, we risk rewarding resource scaling or engineering shortcuts rather than genuine algorithmic collaboration or improved safety. This subsection synthesizes recurring mismatches observed across retrieval, latent‑communication, memory and safety evaluation literatures and proposes a compact, operational reporting standard to enable fair head‑to‑head assessment. We also introduce the Collaboration Gain (Γ) concept to isolate intrinsic collaborative value from budget increases, and give practical recipes for factor attribution and pooled evaluation.

Common sources of mismatch and their consequences

- Index size, compression and multi‑vector footprints. Retrieval and memory systems trade storage for effectiveness. Late‑interaction retrievers such as [37] often use multi‑vector or token‑level representations that increase storage relative to single‑vector bi‑encoders. Papers that omit index footprint can therefore hide gains that are purely a function of larger storage. Works that report compression and storage tradeoffs—e.g., [37] and [36]—remind us to treat index bytes per document, number of vectors per document, and compression scheme (PQ/residual/centroid quantization) as primary experimental variables alongside accuracy.

- Index freshness and negative‑sampling cadence. Retrieval training is sensitive to how often the index and negatives are refreshed. Methods that interleave ANN refresh with model updates, such as [40], produce different dynamics from approaches that use static lexical negatives or light disagreement mining like [36]. When two papers use different negative‑sampling regimes, reported improvements may reflect stale‑baseline pathologies rather than model superiority. Evaluation should therefore disclose negative‑mining schedules, index refresh cadence (wall‑clock frequency and trigger rules), and whether hard negatives come from a dynamic ANN index or fixed lexical baselines.

- Hardware and prefill / KV‑cache access. Agent pipelines often exploit low‑level runtime features such as past_key_values access, RoPE internals, or coprocessor offload. Techniques that reuse cached K/V state can yield large latency and token‑cost improvements. For example, [29] reports significant prefill speedups via KV reuse, and training‑free latent communication proposals such as [1] rely on homogeneous checkpoints to be effective. Comparisons that do not match hardware, inference engine, and KV affordances are inherently mismatched. Papers should report GPU/CPU models, inference engine versions, prefill strategy, whether KV caches were used and reused, and any anchor pools or RoPE offset machinery employed.

- Judging protocol: prompts, temperature and CoT. Many safety and trajectory evaluations depend on LLM judges. Small changes in judge prompts, chain‑of‑thought flags, or temperature can materially alter judgments. Works such as [38] and [2] show that robust evaluation needs careful judge prompt design and calibration. Benchmarks that report a single judge prompt without human calibration risk opaque evaluations: a defense may appear to win simply because it changes the judge’s view. Evaluations should publish full judge prompts (system role and few‑shot exemplars), temperature and decoding strategy, CoT settings, judge calibration statistics (ECE, AUC vs human labels), and a human audit sample to quantify judge reliability.

- Pooled‑labeling, candidate censorship and evaluation completeness. Pooling bias is a well‑known IR problem: gold labels created from pools of top candidates favor systems that produced those candidates. Agent and safety benchmarks face the same issue. If pooled candidates come only from a narrow set of systems, other systems will be unfairly penalized. Pooled labeling must therefore collect outputs across all evaluated systems and tools; benchmarks should disclose the pooling procedure, the set of systems pooled, sampling budgets, and human annotation quotas. When full pooling is infeasible, authors should provide sensitivity analyses demonstrating the impact of pooling choices.

- Multi‑metric tradeoffs: utility, ASR, latency, MTTR. Single‑number metrics hide critical tradeoffs. A method that improves task utility at the cost of a higher attack surface or longer mean time to remediate (MTTR) may be unsuitable in safety‑critical deployments. Safety‑conscious evaluations should report utility (task success or pass@k), attack success rates (ASR) across threat models (prompt injection, memory poisoning, tool compromise), latency including prefill costs (median and tail), and MTTR measured with standardized incident playbooks. Benchmarks such as [13] and [17] explicitly explore these utility–robustness tradeoffs and show why multi‑axis reporting is essential.

Concrete reporting standards to avoid unfair comparisons

Below is a practical reproducibility and fairness checklist that should accompany any head‑to‑head claim in agent research.

1. Resource and budget accounting
   - Report total inference budget and how it was computed: wall‑clock time per request, total decode FLOP estimate, and/or monetary API cost for training and evaluation. Sum budgets across agents for MAS comparisons and include a matched single‑agent baseline under the same aggregate budget (see Collaboration Gain Γ).
   - Include hardware details: GPU/CPU model names, inference engine versions (e.g., vLLM, Triton), and memory bandwidth. For KV reuse or coprocessor work, state whether past_key_values and RoPE internals were accessible.

2. Retrieval & index metadata
   - Provide corpus size (documents/chunks), chunking heuristics (token window and stride), embedding model checkpoint, index library and topology (FAISS/Annoy/sqlite‑vec), vector dimensionality, product quantization details (bits per subvector), and bytes per document.
   - State number of vectors per document, token‑level late‑interaction footprints, and compression strategies (e.g., residual quantization).

3. Negative‑sampling & training regimen
   - Describe negative mining recipe: static or dynamic, refresh schedule, negative pool size per query, and whether hard negatives came from ANN or cross‑encoder re‑ranking.
   - If a late‑interaction reranker was used, report the additional compute cost.

4. Inference / KV‑cache primitives and assumptions
   - State whether evaluations used training‑free KV concatenation or trained coprocessors, whether checkpoints were homogeneous (required for lossless KV concat per [1]), and whether RoPE re‑indexing or anchor pools (e.g., [29]) were used.
   - If a method depends on internal state access (hidden layers, past_key_values), declare that dependency and provide fallback evaluations when that access is unavailable.

5. Judge prompts, calibration and human audit budgets
   - Publish judge prompts verbatim (system role and few‑shot exemplars), temperature, decoding strategy, CoT toggles, and judge checkpoint(s).
   - Report judge calibration metrics (ECE, ROC/AUC vs human labels) and ensemble composition. Always sample a fixed human audit budget (e.g., 5% of cases but at least 200 instances) stratified by difficulty, following practices in [2].

6. Pooling & annotation protocols
   - Pool outputs across all evaluated systems (top‑k per system) before human annotation, disclose the pooling set and candidate counts, and document the sampling strategy. If pooling is infeasible, provide principled justification and sensitivity analyses.

7. Multi‑metric dashboard
   - Publish at minimum: utility (task success/pass@k), ASR per attack family, latency (prefill + decode median/95th), index footprint (GB), and MTTR (detection + remediation).
   - For safety evaluations, report ASR under adaptive adversaries and show defense cost in utility and latency.

Isolating collaboration value: Collaboration Gain Γ and factor attribution

MAS vs single‑agent comparisons are especially vulnerable to confounding by budget differences. A multi‑agent system can appear superior simply because it aggregates more compute, context, or index capacity. To quantify intrinsic collaborative value, use Collaboration Gain Γ as follows (following the recommendation in [89]):

- Let Φ(X, B) be a performance scalar (e.g., success rate) of system X under resource budget B. Choose B as a single, transparent currency (monetary cost per request or FLOP equivalent) and publish the accounting.
- Define Γ(B) = Φ(M, B) / Φ(S, B), where M is the MAS and S is a single‑agent reference given the same budget B. If Γ(B) > 1, the MAS yields improvement beyond what an equally‑resourced single agent can achieve.

Practical protocol to compute Γ and attribute factors:
1. Select B (e.g., total inference USD per request).
2. Build a single‑agent baseline S that consumes the same aggregate budget B—this may require scaling the single agent’s model size, KV budget, or decoding steps. Role‑play single‑agent emulations like [7] are useful baselines.
3. Measure Φ for both systems on the same test set and compute Γ(B).
4. Run controlled ablations on resource and design factors—index size (I), refresh cadence (r), hardware stack (h), judge prompt (j), pooling choices (p)—to estimate each factor’s marginal effect on Φ. A small factorial experiment or regression can efficiently decompose drivers of Γ.

Examples and cautionary tales

- Retrieval index size vs quality. Comparing a MAS that uses multi‑vector late interaction with a single‑vector baseline without reporting index size conflates storage with algorithmic gains. [37] provides explicit compression experiments to guide fair comparisons.

- Negative sampling and training stability. Methods that use dynamic ANN negatives such as [40] will improve faster under frequent refresh; comparing them to static BM25 baselines without equalizing refresh cadence is unfair.

- KV primitives and reproducing latency wins. Claims of token/latency wins from KV reuse must disclose whether anchor pools or RoPE de‑rotation approximations like [29] were used. If a competitor lacks past_key_values access, provide an alternative baseline (e.g., a single‑agent role‑play that reuses KV caches as in [7]).

- Judge prompt fragility. Safety claims validated only against a single, uncalibrated LLM judge are fragile. [2] and [38] show that ensembles plus human verification materially change safety assessments. Publish automated judge outputs and the human audit subset, and report judge–human agreement.

- Coverage gaps and meta‑auditing. A benchmark can be “solved” yet fail in practice because it lacks coverage. [38] demonstrates how meta‑auditing uncovers residual unsafe behaviors. Include a meta‑audit step and report coverage diagnostics (for example, an uncovered‑rate for enumerator budgets).

Meta‑audit and benchmark completeness

A practical meta‑audit procedure: enumerate workflow families (tool calls, memory writes, nested planners), generate concrete instances per family with varied surface realizations (UI changes, wording, file formats), run agents to generate failures, and compute a coverage metric—the fraction of derived rules that had at least one failing instance. Automated enumerators combined with a rule‑resistance metric, as in [38], reveal systematic blind spots. Report enumerator budgets and uncovered‑rate so readers understand residual risk.

Designing multi‑metric dashboards and reporting MTTR

A usable dashboard should present axes that let practitioners judge operational and safety tradeoffs. At minimum report:

- Utility: task success / pass@k with per‑task distributions and confidence.
- ASR family: attack success rates decomposed by surface (prompt injection, memory poisoning, tool misuse), preferably under adaptive adversaries.
- Latency: prefill and decode times with median and tail.
- Index footprint: GB and vectors per document.
- Judge reliability: ECE and judge–human agreement over sampled audits.
- MTTR: time to detect (T_detect) and time to remediate (T_remediate), reported separately and summed as MTTR.

Operationalizing MTTR requires standardized incident playbooks. For example, a guard that detects poisoning in 30 s but needs 10 minutes to remediate may be less desirable than a slower detector that triggers an automated 1‑minute remediation. Report detection and remediation components explicitly.

Example checklists and minimal tables to accompany papers

In an appendix table, include:

- Budget accounting: B (USD per request), FLOP estimate and computation method.
- Hardware: GPU/CPU, vLLM/Triton versions, prefill configuration.
- Index metadata: corpus size, chunk size, vectors per doc, embedding dim, index bytes, PQ bits, library and parameters.
- Negative mining: algorithm (ANN/BM25/disagreement), refresh cadence, negative pool top‑k.
- KV/latent access: past_key_values usage, RoPE compatibility, anchor pool size, homogeneity constraints.
- Judge prompts: full text, temperature, CoT mode, judge models, ensemble composition, human audit size and sampling policy.
- Pooling: systems pooled and sampling budget for annotation.
- Multi‑metric snapshot: utility, ASR per family, latency med/95th, index GB, MTTR under incident playbook.

Applications and case studies that follow parts of this rubric already exist. For example, [36] reports index footprint and disagreement mining; [37] details compression tradeoffs; [29] documents anchor‑based reuse and the homogeneity assumptions for safe KV reuse; and [7] demonstrates how role‑play single‑agent baselines can recover much of the MAS benefit under certain assumptions. On the safety side, [38], [21], [22], [17], and [35] show how richer threat models and longer trajectories change conclusions about defense efficacy. Action‑level guards such as those studied in [51] and [16] quantify guard overheads and underscore the need to report utility‑vs‑safety tradeoffs.

Conclusions and directions for the community

Benchmarks are not neutral. Unless the community converges on richer reporting standards, many claims will remain ambiguous: is an improvement due to a better algorithm, a bigger cache, a deeper index, a changed judge prompt, or simply a larger budget? To make the science cumulative we recommend:

- Treat resource accounting as primary. Report budgets in a standardized currency (USD per request and per‑request FLOPs) and always compute Γ to determine whether collaboration adds intrinsic value beyond budget increases.
- Make retrieval and index metadata visible. Index bytes, vectors per document, PQ bits, and refresh cadence are necessary to interpret IR and memory results.
- Publish judge prompts, calibration statistics and human audit budgets. Transparent judges and human calibration make safety claims reproducible.
- Pool widely. Pool outputs across all systems before labeling and disclose pooling policies and budgets; report pool coverage diagnostics and run meta‑audits where feasible.
- Report multi‑metric dashboards. Publish utility, ASR per attack family, prefill+decode latency med/95th, index footprint and MTTR. Provide an ablation table that quantifies which components drive measured gains.

If these conventions are adopted, future papers will be judged not just by headline accuracy but by transparent budgets, index mechanics, judge fidelity and robustness portfolios. That will enable stronger research questions: when Γ(B) remains high after budget normalization, which mechanisms (latents, explicit role decomposition, memory consolidation) account for the improvement? When defense X lowers ASR but raises MTTR, how should we choose operating points in safety‑sensitive domains? The literature already provides starting points—[89] articulates Γ as a standard, [1] and [29] expose critical low‑level assumptions, and retrieval works such as [37], [40], and [36] show how index engineering and negative mining shape outcomes. Adopting these reporting practices will make comparisons meaningful and accelerate principled progress toward safer, more efficient, and genuinely collaborative agentic intelligence.

### 10.6. Practical decision checklist and deployment playbook
This subsection translates the technical and experimental literature into a concrete operational playbook for deploying LLM-based agents and multi-agent systems (MAS) with memory, latent communication, and runtime guards. It synthesizes recurring engineering patterns and failure modes and distills them into actionable decision rules, numeric thresholds, and a runbook that practitioners can adopt immediately. The aim is to bridge two recurring gaps across papers: (1) the tension between efficiency (KV reuse, latent channels, coprocessors) and auditability/robustness; and (2) the need for practical, empirically grounded deployment recipes that close the loop from red-team findings to operational controls and MTTR targets. Throughout we cite representative studies as evidence for recommended mechanisms and failure cases (memory poisoning, step-level tool misinvocations, documentation-embedded injections). We then provide a 15‑point checklist, a decision flow (narrative flowchart), concrete numeric thresholds, and minimal pseudocode and metric checklists for on-call readiness.

High-level principles that underlie the checklist
- Fail-safe over fail-fast alone: prefer graded responses (redaction, paraphrase, reduced capability) and containment before blunt aborts. The adaptive disclosure and shaping idea in [34] supports guard designs that attempt safe continuation rather than immediate termination.
- Minimize the attack surface at planner and memory-write points: corrupting upstream planner prompts or persistent memories produces outsized downstream harm. This principle is repeatedly emphasized in works such as [33] and [53].
- Layer and diversify judgment: single LLM judges are brittle. Ensembles, consensus mechanisms, and specialized validators (step-level guards, execution verifiers) substantially reduce false negatives in practice; see [2], [10], and [72].
- Require evidence-backed memory admission and conservative consolidation: admit long-term memories only after corroboration, execution verification, or human confirmation. See [11] and [10].
- Use canaries, low-privilege sandboxes, and staged rollouts for high-risk capabilities and memory-schema changes. Canarying combined with judge-sampled audits and rollback hooks reduces MTTR and blast radius; see [44] and [72].

The 15-point engineering checklist (compact, actionable)
Each item below includes a short rationale and concrete thresholds or fallbacks informed by the literature.

1) Classify capability risk and assign capability manifests before deployment
- Action: For every tool, memory store, or high-privilege action define a capability manifest describing scope, TTL, max_invocations_per_hour, required provenance tokens, and human-approval levels.
- Rationale: Capability manifests make platform intent explicit and enforceable; advocated in [72] and systematization work such as [33].
- Practical thresholds: Tools capable of destructive actions or exfiltration should require a capability token TTL ≤ 24 h and human approval for the first five invocations.

2) Memory admission rule: “evidence-first” admission with multi-source corroboration
- Action: Admit a memory into persistent episodic/semantic stores only if (a) it is execution-verified OR (b) it has N independent corroborations (default N = 2) from distinct signaled sources (simulator run, validator agent, or human); otherwise write the item to a quarantined staging area with limited TTL.
- Rationale: Memory poisoning and misevolution are high-impact, slow-burn attacks; dual-memory and consensus-based defenses reduce attack success in experiments (see [10] and [11]).
- Default numeric rules: N = 2 for low-privilege writes; require N = 3 or execution verification for writes that alter planner governing policies or capability manifests.

3) Memory lifecycle: versioned records, provenance tokens, and rollback APIs
- Action: Every memory record must include signed provenance (author, source_hash, evidence_refs, TTL, admission_policy). Provide atomic rollback and quarantine endpoints accessible to ops and the safety team.
- Rationale: Signed provenance and explicit rollback reduce blast radius and accelerate forensic triage; see [34] and [72].
- Operational defaults: TTL = 30 days for episodic records; semantic distilled guidelines re-evaluate at 90 days unless refreshed.

4) KV-cache and latent-exchange fallbacks: prefer training-free reuse but detect heterogeneity
- Action: If agents share identical checkpoints and RoPE/positional encodings, permit lossless KV-swap reuse as a fast path. Otherwise use an adapter pathway and, if adapters fail, re-prefill the context from scratch.
- Rationale: Training-free KV reuse gives large token and latency savings, but heterogeneity or RoPE mismatch can corrupt positional semantics. Single-agent emulation is a strong baseline when heterogeneity exists; see [7] and [6].
- Fallback rule: if model_entity ≠ expected_entity or RoPE_similarity < 0.9, disable KV reuse and re-prefill.

5) Coprocessor invocation and latency budgets
- Action: Gate coprocessor or latent-injection calls behind an uncertainty/utility threshold and strict p95 latency budgets. Provide a textual fallback if the coprocessor is unavailable or slow.
- Rationale: Trainable coprocessors and learned latent weavers improve reasoning quality but add latency and a new attack surface; deploy them on demand with clear fallbacks; see [3] and [12].
- Numeric recipe: interactive T_max = 300 ms (p95); non-interactive T_max = 1500 ms. Throttle coprocessor calls per agent to ≤ 10/min in public services.

6) Pre-execution guardrail thresholds for tool calls
- Action: For each proposed tool call compute a harm-risk score H (ensemble of guard models). If H ≥ 0.75 → block and require human override; if 0.4 ≤ H < 0.75 → append guard feedback to agent and require re-plan; if H < 0.4 → allow.
- Rationale: Step-level guard models that provide corrective feedback (rather than blunt aborts) reduce harmful tool invocations while preserving utility. This pattern is documented in [16].
- Empirical evidence: guard + feedback systems have shown large reductions in harmful calls while maintaining benign utility.

7) Judge ensemble sampling policy and calibration SLOs
- Action: Maintain a judged ensemble diverse across model families and symbolic validators. Sample a fraction p_sample = 5–10% of runtime decisions to the ensemble for calibration. Increase sampling to 20–30% during canaries and after memory or planner schema changes.
- Rationale: LLM judges have bias and calibration issues; ensemble sampling plus periodic human audits detect drift and improve calibration. See [2].
- Calibration targets: aim for low expected calibration error (ECE) on held-out audit sets (for example, ECE ≤ 0.05).

8) Canary rollout recipes and traffic schedules
- Action: Use a multi-stage canary: stage 0 (synthetic probes + local tests), stage 1 (1% live traffic + ensemble sampling + 10% human audit of flagged events), stage 2 (10% traffic, 20% sampling), stage 3 (50%), then full rollout. Abort and rollback on ASR > 2× baseline, MTTR degradation, or safety-judge disagreement > 10% absolute.
- Rationale: Staged rollouts with adversarial probes and judge sampling reduce surprise failures; this cadence is supported by [44] and [72].
- Canary thresholds: start at 1%; escalate to 10% after 24–72 hours of stability; require zero critical security incidents in stage 1.

9) Monitor SLOs and define alerting buckets (engineer/ops/safety)
- Action: Track guard_invocation_rate, guard_block_rate, guard_false_positive_rate, judge_disagreement_rate, memory_admission_rate, memory_quarantine_rate, KV_reuse_failure_rate, coprocessor_latency_p95, canary_ASR_delta. Define tiered alerts: P1 (security/high-privilege misuse), P2 (memory poisoning candidate), P3 (latency regressions).
- Rationale: Continuous monitoring and clear triage rules compress MTTR. See the attack-class analyses in [35] and [17].
- Example alert thresholds: guard_block_rate > 0.5% is tolerable if FP_rate ≤ 5%; if FP_rate > 10% adjust thresholds. MTTR SLOs: P1 < 5 minutes; P2 < 30 minutes.

10) Incident runbook for memory poisoning or planner compromise
- Action: A compact runbook:
  1. Isolate write paths and block writes to affected memory(s).
  2. Freeze capability manifests and revoke implicated tokens.
  3. Create read-only snapshots for forensics.
  4. Run consensus re-evaluation (n_rechecks = 3) using judge ensemble + execution replays.
  5. If poisoning confirmed: quarantine affected memories, prune dependent items, rollback to last clean snapshot, and notify stakeholders.
- Rationale: Consensus and lesson-memory patterns speed detection and containment; see [10] and [72].
- Target MTTR: initial containment actions within 5 minutes for high-privilege incidents.

11) Canary & rollback automation plus human-in-the-loop escalation
- Action: Automate rollback triggers tied to ASR or judge disagreement. Pause and escalate to human inspection automatically on any P1 alert during canary windows.
- Rationale: Quick rollback reduces blast radius; staged rollouts and human-in-the-loop inspection are recommended by systematization and platform papers.

12) On-call metric dashboard and minimum commands
- Action: Provide a compact dashboard with time series for guard_invocation_rate, guard_block_rate, judge_disagreement_rate, KV_reuse_failure, coprocessor_latency_p95, memory_quarantine_count, canary_ASR_delta, and MTTR. On-call CLI should support snapshot-state, quarantine-memory, set-capability-ttl, rotate-capability, rollback-memory-snapshot, and set-guard-threshold commands.
- Rationale: Practitioners need small, reliable toolchains for rapid triage; operational studies and audit papers emphasize the value of reproducible triage procedures (see [2]).

13) Judge ensemble design and human audit cadence
- Action: Build heterogeneous ensembles (at minimum three components: one proprietary LLM, one open LLM, and one symbolic validator). For high-risk domains, set human-audit sampling to 5–10% of flagged incidents; for low-risk domains, 1–2%.
- Rationale: Ensemble diversity mitigates correlated failure modes and human audits correct judge drift; see [2].
- Sampling rule: if judge_confidence ∈ [0.4, 0.7], route to human review at 100% for the first 48 hours after a model or feature change.

14) Regular red-team and meta-audit cycles
- Action: Schedule red-team cycles (monthly light, quarterly full) that include README/document-injection tests, UI-affordance persuasion attacks, and long-horizon delayed-trigger trajectories. Use meta-audit enumerators to expand coverage automatically.
- Rationale: Benchmarks such as [53], [18], and [38] show how meta-audits uncover blind spots missed by static test suites.
- Minimum schedule: one light red-team per month; one full meta-audit per quarter.

15) Continuous improvement loop: measure, retrain, and tune conservatively
- Action: Use logged incidents and human audit labels to refine guard models, tune admission policies, and update capability manifests. Enforce a “policy freeze window” (24–72 h) after a patch when judge sampling is increased.
- Rationale: Preference-based RL and supervised retraining are effective but must be staged with canaries and human checks; see related iterative improvement studies and audit papers.

Decision flow (narrative flowchart)
This is a linearized runtime decision flow for a request or proposed action, mapping the checklist above into a practical path.

1. Ingest and contextualize the request (entity, role, session history, memory retrieval).
   - Reference: context-aware access-control ideas in [34].

2. Classify requested capability against manifest (low/medium/high risk).
   - If high risk → require a valid provenance token and human approval for first N invocations; otherwise proceed.

3. Fetch relevant memories (working → episodic → semantic).
   - Apply memory admission filters for any new writes (evidence-first).
   - If retrieved entries have low provenance/confidence, route to quarantine-check or trigger consensus re-evaluation.

4. Planner produces an action plan. Compute plan uncertainty U and utility V.
   - If U × V exceeds the coprocessor threshold → invoke latent coprocessor (subject to rate_limit & latency budget). If unavailable or too slow, fall back to textual CoT or single-agent emulation ([3]).

5. For each proposed tool-call step:
   - Evaluate pre-execution guard H using the fast guard ensemble.
   - If H ≥ 0.75 → block, log, notify human and require override.
   - If 0.4 ≤ H < 0.75 → append guard feedback, require re-plan, and optionally escalate to a stronger guard.
   - If H < 0.4 → allow, with sampled auditing as per judge sampling policy.

6. Commit action; record signed provenance tokens for the action and any new memory writes.
   - If a memory write is high-privilege, place it in staging and require execution verification before durable admission.

7. Monitor post-execution telemetry for deviations or delayed triggers (use delayed-trigger detectors).
   - If suspicious patterns emerge, invoke the incident runbook.

Quantitative thresholds and concrete recipes (summary)
- Guard pre-execution blocks: H ≥ 0.75 (hard block); 0.4 ≤ H < 0.75 (guard + re-plan); H < 0.4 (allow + sampling).
- Coprocessor latency budgets: interactive T_max = 300 ms (p95); non-interactive T_max = 1500 ms.
- KV-reuse gate: RoPE_similarity ≥ 0.9 and identical checkpoint → reuse; else adapter or re-prefill.
- Memory admission corroboration: N = 2 corroborators for low risk; N = 3 or execution-verified for high risk.
- Canary ramp: 1% → 10% → 50% → 100% with 24–72 h stability windows per stage.
- Judge ensemble sampling: p_sample = 5–10% baseline; 20–30% during canaries; route all H ∈ [0.4, 0.7] to human review for 48 h after changes.
- MTTR SLOs: P1 incidents contained < 5 min; P2 < 30 min.
- Memory quarantine TTL: staging 24–72 h; persistent quarantine until human review for P1 writes.

Minimal on-call code/metric checklists (pseudocode sketches)
These sketches map core checks to code-level assertions; integrate them into your monitoring framework.

- Memory admission sketch:
  code:
    if write.is_high_privilege():
        if not write.execution_verified():
            if write.corroborator_count()  0.005 and guard_FP_rate(window) > 0.10:
        alert("guard_too_negative", severity="P2")

- Coprocessor latency fallback:
  code:
    start = now()
    result = call_coprocessor()
    dur = now() - start
    if dur > T_max and fallback_enabled:
        log("coprocessor_timeout", dur)
        run_textual_CoT_fallback()

Operational runbook template (on-call procedures)
- Trigger: any P1 alert (high-privilege tool misuse or memory-poisoning candidate).
- Immediate actions (0–5 minutes):
  1. Isolate: disable writes to affected memory(s) and block implicated capability IDs.
  2. Snapshot: create a read-only snapshot and increase judge sampling to 100% for canary traffic.
  3. Triage: run consensus re-evaluation on suspect memories using at least three independent reasoning paths (LLM judge + symbolic validator + execution replay).
- Containment (5–30 minutes):
  4. If poisoning confirmed: quarantine affected memories; rollback to last clean snapshot; rotate capability tokens and sign new manifests.
  5. Communicate an incident summary with root cause, containment actions, and next steps.
- Recovery and root-cause (30 minutes–4 hours):
  6. Replay traces offline with red-team augmentation to validate the fix.
  7. Update guard thresholds, memory admission policies, and canary schedules; run 24–72 h increased sampling.
  8. Perform a meta-audit to enumerate related scenarios and schedule retraining or policy amendments.

How specific papers inform these rules
- Access control as adaptive disclosure: [34] motivates graded admission and shaping rules and evidence-first admission.
- Multi-agent orchestration and safeguard patterns: [44] demonstrates reviewer-agent safeguard patterns and justifies staged canaries and guard-feedback replan flows.
- Memory poisoning and consensus defenses: [10] empirically shows the value of consensus and lesson memories, motivating quarantined staging writes and consensus re-evaluation.
- Memory misevolution and dual-memory systems: [11] motivates separating executor memory from evaluator memory and adding admission filters.
- Step-level tool guard efficacy and feedback approaches: [16] shows large reductions in harmful tool calls with corrective guard feedback.
- Judge ensembles and memory-augmented auditing: [2] shows that retrieval-augmented CoT evaluators plus ensemble designs produce high-quality automated judgments and guide human-audit budgets.
- Realistic adversarial surfaces and benchmarks: [35], [17], and [21] indicate which adversarial probes and long-horizon trajectories to include in red teams and meta-audits.
- README/documentation injection and the trusted-executor dilemma: [53] documents high exfiltration rates via documentation-instructions and motivates “no automatic-follow docs” rules plus forced human confirmation for privileged operations.
- Latent deliberation and coprocessor trade-offs: [3] and [12] detail benefits and latency/interpretability trade-offs of latent augmentation and inform coprocessor invocation thresholds and fallbacks.
- Single-LLM emulation and KV reuse: [7] and [6] provide guidance on when KV-cache reuse and single-LLM role-play outperform multi-instance MAS.
- Step-level misalignment detection and corrective policies: [51] supports iterative correction over blunt aborts.
- Governance and capability manifests: systematization and platform works justify capability manifests, runtime revocation, and registry-level enforcement.

Guidelines for tuning and safety trade-offs
- When to widen guard bands: in high-utility domains with frequent benign tool use, prefer H ∈ [0.4, 0.75] with heavier guard feedback and invest in judge ensemble sampling to control false positives. For high-stakes domains (finance, infrastructure) tighten thresholds and increase human audits.
- Latency vs. fidelity for coprocessors: if user latency SLOs are strict, reduce coprocessor usage and rely on single-LLM deliberation plus stricter post-execution verification. Reserve coprocessors for offline or asynchronous background refinement when possible.
- KV reuse vs. heterogeneity: use KV reuse and latent exchanges only when homogeneity is guaranteed; otherwise invest in adapters or accept re-prefill costs as insurance against silent RoPE/positional mismatch failures.

Concluding practical notes and a short checklist for first-day readiness
- Minimum Day 0 deploy checklist:
  1. Deploy capability manifests and sign tools.
  2. Enable memory-admission staging (quarantine by default).
  3. Activate pre-execution guard with default thresholds (H block ≥ 0.75).
  4. Start judge ensemble with p_sample = 10% and an ECE dashboard.
  5. Prepare canary automation and run an initial 1% canary with synthetic red-team probes.
  6. Provision an incident runbook, essential CLI commands, and SRE on-call rotation with MTTR targets.

- Minimum on-call metric list:
  - guard_invocation_rate (per minute)
  - guard_block_rate (per minute)
  - guard_false_positive_rate (rolling 1 h)
  - judge_disagreement_rate (rolling 15 m)
  - memory_admission_rate & memory_quarantine_rate
  - KV_reuse_failure_rate & coprocessor_latency_p95
  - canary_ASR_delta & canary_judge_flags

Closing synthesis
This playbook synthesizes practical trade-offs and engineering controls documented across a large body of recent work. Recurring themes are clear: protecting the planner and memory-write points yields high defensive return; diverse judge ensembles plus evidence-backed memory admission reduce poisoning and misalignment risk; latent and KV-based efficiency mechanisms provide large cost/latency gains but require homogeneity or reliable adapters; and step-level guard models that provide corrective feedback preserve utility while reducing harmful tool invocations. Staged canaries, judge sampling, and human audits compress MTTR and reduce deployment surprise.

These rules should be treated as living defaults. Tune them to domain risk, model families, and operational budgets. The community benefits from sharing canonical artifacts—capability-manifest schemas, canary probe suites, judge prompts, and quarantine/rollback APIs—so teams can reproduce both wins and failure modes. Several papers recommend open benchmark artifacts to accelerate reproducible practice (for example, [2] and [35]). Adopting and contributing to such shared artifacts will narrow the gap between red-team findings and robust, auditable agent deployments.

We organize findings around five interacting axes—latent channels, memory lifecycles, retrieval/indexing, training regimes, and safety defenses—and distill practical, evidence‑backed patterns. Training‑free KV reuse with RoPE‑aware alignment yields substantial token and latency wins under homogeneous checkpoints ([1]), while heterogeneity and specialization benefit from trainable coprocessors and careful retrieval tuning ([3], [36]). Layered consensus/admission defenses and lesson memories markedly reduce memory‑poisoning and harmful tool invocations ([10]). Prioritized call to action: release canonical artifact bundles, standardize judge ensembles and human‑audit budgets, and fund heterogeneity‑focused benchmark suites and toolkits.

- Key takeaways: training‑free KV reuse + RoPE alignment for homogeneous settings; trainable coprocessors and tuned retrieval for heterogeneity; consensus + lesson admission for safety ([1], [3], [10]). 
- Reproducibility & artifact readiness: see the Artifact Annex for the canonical artifact bundle and reporting matrix (anchor pools, index snapshots, coprocessor hashes, judge prompts). 
- Limitations of the survey: scope is English‑dominant and simulator‑heavy, with heterogeneous evaluation protocols and reliance on preprints/closed models. 
- Practical checklist: see the Deployment Playbook for the prioritized 10‑point operational checklist (planner hardening, consensus admission, dual memories, judge ensembles, KV fallbacks).


## 11. Key takeaways
The survey’s technical threads converge on a practical set of engineering truths: some interventions deliver large, reliable wins with modest complexity; others offer asymmetric payoffs at the cost of new attack surfaces or operational burdens. Below I synthesize the most robust takeaways, explain the invariants under which they hold (and where they break), and link prescriptions to representative evidence. Wherever possible I emphasize measurable trade-offs (compute, tokens, latency, interpretability, attack surface) and provide a short decision logic for selecting patterns in production agentic stacks.

Concise operational summary (what works, what is risky)
- Training‑free KV‑cache concatenation, combined with RoPE‑aware alignment and anchor‑based reuse, yields dramatic token and latency reductions when you can access model internals and run homogeneous checkpoints. Empirical pipelines that exchange layerwise KV state report order‑of‑magnitude prefill savings and several‑fold end‑to‑end speedups without retraining, but these approaches assume access to past_key_values and compatible positional encodings ([1], [6]).
- Small, local retriever refinement guided by cheap self‑supervision (disagreement mining) is an outsized win: disagreement‑mined negatives plus a short MultipleNegativesRankingLoss fine‑tune often matches or approaches heavier late‑interaction retrievers at a fraction of index size and tuning cost ([36], [37]).
- Latent (machine‑native) memories and in‑flow latent deliberation substantially reduce token budgets and inference latency, enabling richer internal deliberation and multi‑hypothesis encoding; they also shrink end‑to‑end compute but undermine auditability and invite new poisoning modes that textual memories avoid ([3], [1]).
- Consensus‑based runtime validation, a separate “lesson” memory for distilled corrections, and step‑level pre‑execution guardrails that provide corrective feedback rather than binary aborts sharply reduce harmful tool invocations while preserving benign utility; these defenses impose modest latency and token overhead relative to blunt red‑team mitigations ([16], [51]).
- Reproducible benchmarks must reflect long‑horizon, tool‑mediated, memory‑dependent interactions. Reproducing reported safety or robustness gains requires matching index size/refresh budgets, anchor‑pool maintenance, judge prompts, and negative‑cadence choices ([2], [35]).

Latent communication and KV engineering: when training‑free wins and why
A striking, recurring finding is the scale of practical gains when agents exchange model‑native state rather than verbose textual messages. The training‑free, layerwise KV concatenation protocol—emit last‑layer “latent thoughts,” map them back via a lightweight alignment projector, and concatenate layerwise past_key_values into a successor agent—delivers lossless cross‑agent conditioning and large token savings under the homogeneous‑checkpoint assumption ([1]). Organizer/primitive architectures that compose small, reusable latent operators (Review, Voting, Planning) and orchestrate them with a lightweight Organizer obtain similar throughput gains: exchanging KV fragments instead of natural language reduces token counts and latencies by factors of 3–4 in reported settings ([6], [1]).

Even when exact, lossless concatenation is impossible, training‑free engineering can recover much of the benefit. Anchor‑pool methods that de‑rotate RoPE‑encoded keys and interpolate empirically observed offsets allow reuse across slightly differing prefixes, skipping expensive full prefills and producing substantial prefill speedups while preserving quality ([29]). These results are robust: if internals are available and models share architecture and positional encodings, prefill savings are an immediate, low‑friction win—no retraining required.

The caveat is sharp. Training‑free KV concatenation and anchor reuse depend on homogeneity: identical checkpoints (or faithful adapters) and compatible RoPE/positional encodings. When those invariants fail—heterogeneous expert pools, differing RoPE implementations, or divergent layer shapes—naïve concatenation can corrupt positional semantics and produce silent failures. Per‑layer adapters and projection matrices mitigate some mismatch but reintroduce training and calibration costs ([6]). In short: adopt training‑free KV tricks when you control checkpoints or can afford adapter investment; otherwise prefer conservative textual protocols or hybrid designs.

Training‑free versus trainable latent designs: rules of thumb
Two families of latent augmentation compete: (i) training‑free KV concatenation, closed‑form alignment and anchor interpolation; and (ii) trained coprocessors, learned soft‑token injectors, and staged curricula that co‑train to generate specialized latents. Both are valuable but differ in sweet spots.

- Training‑free protocols excel when you can run homogeneous checkpoints and want immediate latency/token wins. They are robust to limited data, require no fine‑tuning, and produce predictable throughput improvements ([1], [6]).

- Trainable coprocessors and learned weavers are preferable when heterogeneity, specialization, multimodality, or richer invocation policies are necessary. Training stages (SFT→RL) and modular adapters enable cross‑model mapping, disciplined invocation, and higher peak reasoning quality—at the cost of training, tuning, and the risk of latent redundancy if specialization is not enforced ([3], [12], [46]).

In practice, hybridization is pragmatic: deploy a training‑free baseline for immediate gains, instrument latent channels with diagnostics, and introduce small adapters or targeted training (LoRA/per‑layer projectors) when production heterogeneity or new tasks demand specialization.

Retrieval, negative mining and local‑first engineering: cheap levers with large ROI
Retrieval quality is one of the single largest levers for end‑user utility; modest, local interventions often yield outsized returns. The “local‑first, hybrid fusion” pattern—combine dense vectors with full‑text lexical retrieval and fuse rankings with adaptive per‑query weights—gives robustness to rare surface forms without the index size or runtime costs of full late‑interaction retrievers. Practical pipelines mine hard negatives cheaply by identifying disagreement between dense and lexical retrievals, fine‑tune a compact embedder with MultipleNegativesRankingLoss, and fuse dense and lexical ranks via Reciprocal Rank Fusion with per‑query IDF weighting; this yields strong NDCG improvements with tiny models and low latency ([36], [37]).

By contrast, expensive ANN negative regimes (ANCE‑style) can deliver stronger final models but require frequent full‑corpus reencodes and heavy infrastructure, which can be prohibitive for local or privacy‑sensitive deployments. The practical lesson: when constrained to on‑device or single‑file stores, disagreement‑mined negatives plus a compact retriever fine‑tune are a high‑value, low‑cost pattern that narrows the gap to heavyweight alternatives.

A few engineering knobs merit special attention:
- Late‑interaction compression and denoised distillation can recover much late‑interaction quality with far smaller footprints but incur nontrivial training complexity ([37]).
- Reproducible retrieval claims must report index size, compression, refresh cadence and negative sampling cadence; without these, cross‑paper comparisons are fragile.

Memory architectures and life‑cycle governance: the double‑edged sword
Persistent memory is indispensable for long‑horizon embodied agents, repeatable workflows, and systems that must accumulate procedural knowledge. But memory designs—textual episodic logs, dense 3D snapshot banks, generative latent weaves, and parametric consolidation—impose distinct trade‑offs.

A pragmatic, auditable architecture that recurs across successful systems is a three‑tier memory: working → episodic → semantic. The working buffer holds immediate context; structured episodic representations (trajectories and spatial KGs) provide grounded, retrievable traces; and a distilled semantic store encodes reusable lessons. This pattern balances latency, interpretability and consolidation: episodic graphs preserve provenance and spatial grounding; semantic guidelines are compact and human‑readable for audits; working memory keeps planning prompts compact ([24], [26]).

Dense 3D episodic banks support high‑fidelity visual recall but inflate storage and retrieval costs and often require supervised fusion modules ([19], [45]). Generative latent memories and in‑flow weavers synthesize compact machine‑native token sequences for the decoder to consume. They are efficient and suitable in frozen‑backbone settings, but they complicate interpretability and increase poisoning risk unless admission controls and provenance are enforced ([12], [3]).

Operational practices that reduce misevolution and poisoning risk
Memory admission, consolidation and lifecycle controls are mandatory in practice. Effective operational recipes include:
- Execution‑verified admission: only promote memory entries that pass execution checks or independent validators ([26], [32]).
- Dual‑memory separation: keep fast executor memory for provisional, high‑throughput reads and a vetted evaluator memory for trusted, auditable lessons ([11], [10]).
- Consensus validation: require multiple independent reasoning paths or evaluators to agree before admitting high‑impact memories; consensus reduces poisoning success with modest utility loss.
- Quarantine, mutation tests and rollback: version every memory entry with provenance metadata, hold suspect writes in quarantine, stress them with synthetic prefixes to reveal delayed triggers, and provide explicit rollback APIs.

Defense patterns that work in practice: consensus, lessons and step‑level guards
Two defense patterns consistently reduce harmful tool invocations without wrecking utility:

1) Consensus + lesson memory. Before trusting retrieved memories or enacting high‑risk plans, generate multiple reasoning traces (parallel LLM runs or diverse model paths). Admit memories and execute plans only when consensus or a confidence threshold is met; when failures occur, store a distilled “lesson” separately. This pattern materially reduces memory‑poisoning success while preserving useful capability ([10], [11]).

2) Step‑level guard + guided correction. Instead of blind aborts, apply a fast guard that flags risky tool calls and then a richer second‑stage guard that returns structured corrective feedback, enabling the planner to replan. This guide‑and‑correct approach reduces harmful invocations and maintains throughput, producing audit artifacts that aid governance ([16], [51]).

LLM‑as‑judge, ensembles and judge calibration
Training and defense pipelines depend critically on judge quality. Retrieval‑augmented CoT evaluators can approach human‑level judgment on many labels, but they are brittle to exemplar poisoning and judge bias ([2]). Ensemble judges, judge calibration (ECE), and periodic human audits are pragmatic stabilizers: use a diverse ensemble (different model families and symbolic checks), calibrate against human labels, and make human audit sampling part of routine monitoring before committing memory writes or policy updates.

Interpretability and auditing of latent channels
Latent memories and KV exchanges improve efficiency but reduce transparency. Effective mitigations include:
- Pair each latent write with a concise textual “certificate” that explains why the latent was created and when it should be used ([26]).
- Build light decoders that map latent clusters to textual approximations; store both latent and paraphrase in audit logs for post‑hoc inspection.
- Periodically sample latent traces for human inspection and include them in incident playbooks, using visualizers and attention diagnostics to speed triage ([1]).

Evaluation hygiene and reproducibility constraints
A recurring methodological gap is inconsistent reporting: index size, refresh cadence, anchor‑pool seeds, judge prompts, negative‑mining cadence, and hardware for latency measurement are often omitted. Without standardized disclosure, comparisons are brittle. Minimal reporting must include:
- Retrieval: index bytes per document, vectors per document, compression scheme, index refresh cadence and negative sampling schedule.
- Latent communication: exact checkpoint hashes, layer shapes, RoPE variant, anchor pool details, and alignment projector hyperparameters ([1], [29]).
- Memory defenses: judge prompts, ensemble composition, consensus thresholds, quarantine TTLs and human audit sampling budgets.
- Safety experiments: attacker model definitions, tool fidelity, delayed‑trigger protocols and per‑step logs.

Domain vignettes: how trade‑offs play out
- Embodied robotics: hierarchical symbolic spatial KGs with episodic/semantic consolidation (working→episodic→semantic) give strong ROI—improved long‑horizon planning without heavy LLM finetuning ([4], [19]).
- Private on‑device agents: a local single‑file vector + FTS store with disagreement‑mined negatives and a compact embedder fine‑tune is a practical privacy‑aware pattern ([36]).
- High‑privilege computer‑use agents: documentation‑embedded attacks (README attacks) are highly effective in practice; capability manifests, signed provenance, and explicit confirmation are mandatory controls ([53]).

A concise deployment playbook
1. If you control model internals and agents are homogeneous, prefer training‑free KV concatenation with RoPE‑aware anchor reuse for multi‑agent efficiency; instrument carefully and add adapter fallbacks for heterogeneity ([1], [6]).
2. For constrained deployments, improve retrieval by mining dense–lexical disagreement and fine‑tuning a small embedder; reserve late‑interaction compression for high‑budget server settings ([36], [37]).
3. Use a three‑tier memory (working → episodic → semantic) with execution‑verified admission and dual‑memory separation to prevent misevolution ([26], [10]).
4. Defend memory and tool calls with consensus validation plus a lesson memory and prefer guide‑and‑correct pre‑execution guard behavior; calibrate judge ensembles and sample human audits ([16], [2]).
5. Audit latent channels: store textual provenance alongside machine‑native latents, run periodic decoding probes, and maintain human‑in‑the‑loop inspection for high‑privilege contexts ([3], [12]).
6. Report full experimental context: index size, negative‑cadence, anchor pool seed, judge prompts and model hashes; use long‑horizon trajectory and step‑level benchmarks to surface delayed‑trigger risks ([2], [53]).

Research directions and unresolved bottlenecks
The near‑term engineering terrain is clear: training‑free KV reuse and RoPE‑aware alignment unlock practical gains today; disagreement‑mined negatives and small retriever fine‑tuning are accessible improvements for resource‑constrained settings; latent memories and coprocessors promise richer in‑flow reasoning with large efficiency payoffs; and consensus + lesson defenses plus step‑level guards provide a defensible baseline for reducing tool misuse. Remaining bottlenecks include latent interpretability, robust heterogeneity adapters, judge calibration at scale, and memory admission policies that scale to streaming, multi‑session agents. Progress will require shared benchmarks (trajectory + index + judge artifacts), open anchor pools, and reproducible KV‑communication toolkits so practitioners can replicate both wins and failure modes under matched operational constraints ([2], [1], [29]).

Closing synthesis
When infrastructure and homogeneity hold, training‑free KV concatenation and anchor‑based reuse provide immediate, large efficiency gains; otherwise, invest in small, trainable modules (adapters, routers, coprocessors) and enforce governance via consensus, lesson memories and step‑level guards. Prioritize pragmatic retriever tuning over wholesale finetuning in constrained deployments; treat latent memories as a performance optimization that must be paired with textual provenance and stringent admission controls. Validate safety improvements with trajectory‑level, step‑aware benchmarks and judge calibration rather than relying on pointwise accuracy metrics alone. The field now has a reproducible toolkit of high‑leverage, empirically validated interventions to advance both capability and safety—provided the community adopts rigorous reporting, shared artifacts, and careful operational controls.

### 11.1. Reproducibility and artifact readiness
Reproducibility and artifact readiness are fundamental to turning the recent empirical ferment around LLM-based agents into cumulative science. Practically, this requires releasing a compact, explicit artifact bundle (anchor pools for KV reuse; latent coprocessor and weaver checkpoints; indexed corpora and index snapshots; prompt banks and prompt-version hashes; judge prompts, exemplar banks and calibration artifacts; negative-mining logs and schedules; seed lists and RNG provenance; and runnable safety benchmark harnesses and micro-benchmarks) together with machine-readable reporting tables. Equally important is a minimal, canonical experiment suite for head-to-head comparisons: retrieval ablations (indexed snapshots, ANN params, negative-cadence, chunking rules with train/val/test splits and seeds); KV/anchor reuse micro-benchmarks (anchor-pool sizes, RoPE variants, homogeneity vs heterogeneity baselines); latent-coprocessor injection tests (checkpoint hash, injection positions, latent budgets); memory-admission stress tests (adversarial replay streams, admission-policy sweeps); and safety trajectory matrices (pre-registered judge/clique settings, ensemble size, human-audit fraction, red-team budget). Every published run should report a compact compute and experiment matrix (hardware, wallclock build/re-encode times, index size bytes, median/p95 latency, training updates, negative-cadence hours, anchor-hash, coprocessor-hash, prompt_hashes and seed list) so others can reproduce performance and failure modes precisely. Finally, we call for community-maintained baseline stacks (local hybrid retrieval templates, canonical anchor pools, a latent-coprocessor registry, safety-harness runner and small embodied-memory traces) versioned with immutable hashes and lightweight governance so the field can iterate from stable baselines rather than ad-hoc engineering choices.

Why a standardized artifact bundle matters
Recent evaluation and audit efforts show how subtle engineering choices change measured safety and utility. Meta-audit approaches reveal coverage gaps that only become visible when workflows are enumerated systematically: for example, [38] demonstrated that coverage rises with enumeration budget and that many benchmarks leave more than 20% of unsafe behaviors uncovered if test suites are not grown systematically. Benchmarks that report only aggregate metrics without a full accounting of index budgets, negative-sampling policies, or judge prompts therefore risk overstating robustness. Domain-grounded suites such as [21], [22], and [17] illustrate that long-horizon interactions and delayed-trigger attacks expose failure modes invisible to short-turn tests; reproducing such findings requires full release of trajectories, tool pools, and the harness that executes them. Conversely, domain-specific experimental artifacts matter: reproducing circuit-design claims requires netlists, simulator seeds, PDK/SPICE settings and the exact harness used to parse and validate SPICE outputs (see [63], [32], and [64]).

A canonical artifact checklist
Any reproducible agent paper should release the following minimum artifact bundle. These items are not optional “nice-to-haves”; they materially affect whether other teams can replicate reported gains or failures.

- Core code and exact runtime image:
  - A runnable container/VM image (or Dockerfile + pinned dependency hashes) reproducing the evaluation harness end-to-end. This is critical for systems that exercise external tools, simulators, or OS-level capabilities (cf. the experiment pipelines in [60] and [32]).
  - Exact commit hashes and provenance for models and libraries (model checkpoints, tokenizer versions, ANN libraries such as FAISS/HNSWlib), and the hardware/accelerator configuration used for reported results.

- Model artifacts and checkpoints:
  - For parametric components: checkpoint files (weights) or an explicit persistent identifier (URL, dataset digest) for any third-party model used (foundation LLMs, vision backbones, retrievers). If proprietary LLMs are used, provide a precise API version, API call parameters (temperature, top_p, stopping tokens), and a fallback open checkpoint for reproduction.
  - For latent augmentations and coprocessors: release coprocessor checkpoints (or a canonical seed) and include embedding/hidden-state dimensionality, layer-index mapping, any projectors used to align latents to embedding space, and hyperparameters for soft-token injection (see [3] and [12]).

- Retrieval and index artifacts:
  - A snapshot of the index (or a seed script and the raw documents to rebuild it) plus the index-building configuration: ANN algorithm (HNSW/IVF+PQ), index parameters (nlist, nprobe, PQ bits, centroid+residual parameters), vectorizer model and hyperparameters, chunking rules (token window and overlap), and any compression pipeline (cf. reporting in [37] and the local-first engineering in [36]).
  - If experiments rely on in-production or real-time index rebuilding (e.g., ANCE-style negative refresh), report the refresh cadence, the time required for a full re-encode, and the allowed staleness window (as discussed in [40]).

- Negative mining & training metadata:
  - The negative-sampling schedule used during retriever or contrastive training (static BM25, asynchronous ANN negatives such as ANCE, disagreement-mined negatives, or mixed schedules). Report negative pool size, refresh cadence, and the exact random seeds used when sampling negatives and batching examples (cf. [36]).
  - The loss and hyperparameters (learning rate schedule, batch size, number of updates/epochs, LM temperature/decoding specs) used for any SFT/LoRA/RL stages.

- Prompt artifacts & judge definitions:
  - All prompt templates, including prompt “engineering” used to elicit planning, latent synthesis, or judge outputs. Include prompt-versioning hashes and canonical example sequences to ensure prompts are applied unchanged.
  - Judge prompts, judge model(s), and calibration artifacts. For LLM-based judges this must include the exact prompt used for pairwise comparisons, temperature, chain-of-thought setting (if any), and ensembling rules. Provide the seed list for judge sampling and the human-audit sampling rate (e.g., one human audit per N automated judgments), following practices in [2] and [17] and the dataset release in [53].

- KV / anchor artifacts for latent/KV reuse:
  - Anchor pools used by any KV-prefill/approximation technique (see [29]): publish the anchor set, per-anchor offsets, RoPE-de-rotation parameters and interpolation hyperparameters. Provide an “anchor-hash” and a script to regenerate anchors plus a canonical set of test contexts to measure reuse rate reproducibly.
  - For latent-only communication systems, publish representative KV snapshots (or a reduced reproducible subset) and code to replay them into the target model’s past_key_values layout.

- Memory artifacts and admission logs:
  - For persistent-memory proposals, release the memory-store snapshot (or a tool to seed an empty store) and the exact admission-policy code. Include provenance metadata per entry: timestamp, triggering-context-hash, admission-criterion, supporting-evidence hash, and confidence score, as advocated by memory-governance prototypes such as [11] and [10].

- Test harness and evaluation runner:
  - The harness that runs the experiment and produces evaluation outputs: a standardized runner (containerized) that accepts the above artifacts and produces reported metrics. For safety benchmarks, publish both the automated harness and the human-in-the-loop procedure, including annotator instructions, qualification criteria, and auditing quotas (as in the human-audit practices of [2], [17], and [53]).

Canonical experiments and the minimal reporting matrix
Beyond artifacts, we propose a small set of canonical experiments that every new agent claim should include to enable direct, apples-to-apples comparisons. Each experiment should produce a standard reporting table (structured CSV) for programmatic comparison.

1) Retrieval & negative-sampling ablation suite
Purpose: isolate retrieval/negative-sampling contributions from downstream architecture.
- Design: run retrieval-only experiments on BEIR-style zero-shot tasks ([39]) and on the agent’s domain-specific corpora.
- Variables: embedding model name + commit hash, tokenizer, chunking rules, dimension, ANN algorithm + parameters, index size and latency, negative-sampling policy (ANCE refresh cadence, disagreement-mining parameters as in [36], static BM25, in-batch negatives), training epochs and seed list.
- Report table: dataset, embedding_model, index_type, index_params, index_size_bytes, negative_policy, negative_cadence_hours, training_updates, seed, nDCG@10, recall@k, median_latency_ms, p95_latency_ms.

2) KV/latent reuse and anchor-pool micro-benchmark
Purpose: measure fidelity loss and reuse-rate tradeoffs when approximating or sharing KV caches and latent channels.
- Design: publish a canonical anchor-pool and run:
  - exact prefill baseline (full prefill),
  - anchor-based approximate prefill (as in [1]) with varying anchor sizes and interpolation strengths,
  - latent-coprocessor injection baseline and a no-coprocessor baseline.
- Variables: anchor_pool_size, anchor-selection seed, RoPE variant and de-rotation, model homogeneity (same vs mismatched checkpoint adapter), temperature, approximated-KV threshold.
- Metrics: reuse_rate, decode_drop, perplexity_delta, throughput speedup, wallclock prefill time, reconstruction error for alignment projectors. Report anchor-pool hash and regeneration script.

3) Latent coprocessor checkpoint micro-benchmark
Purpose: allow teams to evaluate shared coprocessor artifacts with diverse decoders and contexts.
- Design: publish a coprocessor checkpoint and a harness that can inject coprocessor outputs into a frozen decoder. Measure perplexity improvements, downstream reasoning gains, and latency/energy tradeoffs.
- Variables: coprocessor_hash, injection_positions, latent_budget, base_decoder_version.
- Report table: coprocessor_hash, base_model_hash, injection_positions, latent_length, perplexity_reduction, pass@k improvement, extra_inference_cpu_ms, extra_inference_gpu_ms.

4) Memory-admission & misevolution stress tests
Purpose: quantify how admission rules, consensus validators, or lesson stores affect utility and safety under adversarial and benign streams.
- Design: replay curated streams mixing benign operations with stealthy memory-poisoning payloads (README-style and contextual injections). Use seeded randomization for multiple replay runs (see [53] and [26]).
- Variables: admission policy (immediate append vs execution-verified write vs consensus filter), dual-memory separation (executor vs evaluator memory as in [11]), memory TTL and pruning cadence.
- Report columns: admission_policy, poisoning_success_rate (ASR), task_success_rate, MTTR_to_detect, memory_store_size, false_positives_in_admission.

5) Safety trajectory and step-level guard comparisons (the safety matrix)
Purpose: evaluate agent behavior across a spectrum of multi-step attacks and intrinsic failures with a consistent judge and human-audit budget.
- Design: select a common suite of trajectory-level benchmarks covering diverse attack modalities and domains: [21], [22], [13], [35], [17], and [53]. Use a fixed judge-prompt bank (release prompt templates and version hash) and a pre-registered human audit sampling rate.
- Variables: red-team budget per trajectory, judge-ensemble size, judge temperature, human audit fraction.
- Report table: benchmark_name, model_name, model_hash, judge_prompt_hash, judge_model, ensemble_size, task_success_rate, trajectory_ASR_by_mode, per-step_localization_F1 (for step-level datasets like [22]), average_latency_per_trajectory, human_audit_fraction, judge_ECE.

6) Embodied memory & long-horizon planning experiments
Purpose: reproduce and compare memory architectures (symbolic KGs, dense 3D episodic banks, latent memories) on common embodied tasks.
- Design: evaluate on canonical embodied suites such as [60], [20], and the long-horizon traces in [21] where possible. For each memory design, measure success rate, goal-conditional success, number of memory writes, retrieval latency, and admission errors.
- Report columns: env_name, agent_variant, memory_tier, memory_format (symbolic KG / dense 3D / latent tokens), consolidation_cadence, SR, GC, memory_write_rate_per_minute, retrieval_median_latency_ms, memory_store_size.
- Rationale: hierarchies and dense episodic banks trade interpretability and storage; publishing micro-benchmarks identifies regimes where symbolic consolidation outperforms dense fusion (cf. [4] versus dense episodic approaches such as [20]).

7) Domain-specific execution-grounded experiments (e.g., circuit/code)
Purpose: make domain claims reproducible by releasing unit-testable artifacts and exact simulator settings.
- Design: release netlists, PySpice scripts, simulator seeds and PDK versions used in experiments reported in executable studies such as [63], [32], and [64].
- Report table: problem_id, model_name, prompt_template_hash, simulator_name_and_version, PDK_or_device_library, seed, pass@1, average_simulations_per_solution, wallclock_time_to_solution, number_of_fix_attempts, final_fom_scores.

Standardized reporting tables (core fields)
We recommend two canonical CSV tables that every agent paper should publish as machine-readable artifacts.

A. Run-level summary table (one row per experimental run)
- run_id, paper_hash, model_name + model_hash, base_seed, hardware_desc, prompt_template_hash, judge_prompt_hash, judge_model + judge_temperature, retrieval_index_hash, anchor_pool_hash, coprocessor_checkpoint_hash, negative_policy, negative_cadence_hours, training_updates, latency_median_ms, latency_p95_ms, task_metric_primary, task_metric_secondary, security_metric_ASR, MTTR_minutes_for_compromise, notes_debug.

B. Index-and-negative-configuration table (one row per index config)
- index_hash, corpus_hash + chunking_rules, embedding_model_name + commit, ANN_algorithm + parameters (JSON), index_size_bytes, PQ_bits, nlist/nprobe or HNSW_m/efConstruction, index_build_time_seconds, negative_policy, negative_pool_size, negative_refresh_cadence_hours, seed_list_snapshot, prefill_time_ms, annotation_notes.

Judge design, calibration, and human-audit budgets
Automated judges are attractive for scale, but their design must be reproducible and calibrated. Practices we recommend:

- Publish judge prompts unambiguously; include exact chain-of-thought directives if used. For ensembles, report each model version and the aggregation rule.
- Report calibration metrics: ECE, ROC-AUC, and human-verified precision/recall on a held-out audit set. This is encouraged by [2].
- Provide an adjudication protocol and a human-audit sampling plan (for example, 5% of automated “safe” judgments and 20% of automated “unsafe” judgments sampled for human review). Publish sampled human judgments and explain the cost/benefit tradeoffs.

Community-maintained baseline stacks and micro-benchmarks
The field will benefit from a small set of community-managed baseline stacks and micro-benchmarks. We recommend a minimal reproducibility stack that is curated and versioned.

- Retrieval baseline stack:
  - Local single-file hybrid retrieval template (SQLite + sqlite-vec + FTS5) with an example anchor pool and re-indexing scripts; an initial tuned embedding model (small BGE-like); scripts to reproduce experiments in [36] and to compare with a ColBERTv2-style late-interaction pipeline ([37]). Provide pre-built index snapshots for a small corpus to allow fast reproduction.

- KV / anchor micro-benchmark:
  - Canonical anchor pools (small/medium/large), replication scripts, and the evaluation harness used in [29]. The harness should support anchor-pool ablations and RoPE offset permutations.

- Coprocessor & latent-checkpoint registry:
  - A registry of latent-coprocessor checkpoints and a canonical injection harness (following [3] and [26]). Each entry should include training data, objective, and a hash.

- Safety benchmark harness:
  - A standardized runner that can execute major safety benchmarks in one place: [21], [22], [13], [35], [17], [53], [51], and the validator examples from [2]. The harness should implement judge prompts and a pluggable human-audit interface so teams can reproduce reported ASR under the same audit budget.

- Embodied & memory micro-bench:
  - A “mini-EmbodiedBench” dataset and harness with a reproducible subset of tasks used in [60] and the memory workflows in [4] and [20].

- Domain execution harness:
  - PySpice + netlist micro-bench harness for electronics tasks reproducing experiments in [63] and [32], with synthetic PDKs or small SPICE decks to avoid licensing issues while preserving evaluation character.

These stacks should be versioned and governed: each baseline artifact (anchor pool, coprocessor checkpoint, index snapshot, judge prompt bank) should have an immutable hash and a meta-file documenting provenance, intended use, and limitations. A pull-request-based governance process will permit incremental improvement while preserving reproducibility.

Meta-audit and coverage reporting
Benchmarks are never complete. As [38] demonstrates, meta-auditing reveals uncovered behavior classes by enumerating workflow families and measuring "rule-resistance." Every benchmark release should provide:

- A meta-audit enumeration script that can extend the test suite by generating abstract workflow patterns and concretizing them into tool-call sequences.
- A coverage report that measures rule-resistance and an “uncovered-rate” metric as a function of enumeration budget.
- A public red-team harness that permits reproducible adversarial upgrades to the benchmark with attribution and human oversight so the community can study adaptive adversaries.

Operational recommendations and pitfalls
Several recurring operational pitfalls materially affect reproducibility; papers should document how they were handled.

- Seed and randomness accounting: experiments that mix sampling-based decodings, ANN approximate searches, and trainer shuffles require reproducible seed chains that cover all random sources. Report the full sequence of seeds and RNGs used in ANN building, shuffling, and LLM sampling.
